title: Still a long way to go before wind forecasting models are as good as they will need to be as installed capacity grows….
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Still a long way to go before wind forecasting models are as good as they will need to be as installed capacity grows…." posted at 27-12-2017 03:42:00 PM is: The news discusses the challenges of accurately forecasting wind energy output as the capacity of wind farms in Australia, particularly South Australia, continues to grow. The complexity of forecasting wind and solar output adds to the difficulty of predicting regional demand within the National Electricity Market (NEM). The article highlights the significant volatility that wind forecasting currently exhibits, causing financial implications due to the need for additional resources to manage unexpected fluctuations. As renewable energy capacity increases, improving forecast accuracy becomes crucial to maintain stable electricity prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Inaccuracy in wind forecasting models. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-12-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia (and Victoria noted for high prices). Its affected key users are: Energy users relying on wind forecasts. Its cause type is: Complexity in forecasting wind outputs. Its causes are: Volatility in wind energy output, increased difficulty in forecasting due to growing renewable capacity.

title: Afternoon “Scheduled Demand” in Victoria on Christmas Day drops as low as overnight demand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Afternoon “Scheduled Demand” in Victoria on Christmas Day drops as low as overnight demand" posted at 26-12-2017 08:37:00 AM is: The article reports on unusually low electricity demand in Victoria on Christmas Day, attributed to moderate temperatures, reduced commercial activity, and significant output from small-scale photovoltaic systems reaching up to 700 MW. These factors combined led to afternoon demand levels comparable to the typically lowest point overnight. Such low demands had traditionally been seen only during nighttime in previous years, but recent trends indicate shifts in demand patterns.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: residential satisfaction. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Moderate temperatures, reduced air-conditioning load, lower commercial usage, and increased small-scale PV output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-12-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial energy consumers. Its cause type is: Demand-side factors. Its causes are: Moderate weather, significant small-scale PV output, reduced commercial activity.

title: Hot weather sees NSW demand at 13,000MW and NEM-wide demand above 31,000MW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hot weather sees NSW demand at 13,000MW and NEM-wide demand above 31,000MW" posted at 19-12-2017 04:18:00 PM is: The article reports a significant rise in electricity demand due to hot weather, with NSW demand reaching up to 13,500MW and NEM-wide demand surpassing 31,000MW, marking a peak not routinely seen in recent summers. The high demand led to increased dispatch prices, particularly in the NSW+VIC+TAS "Economic Island" due to transmission congestion, although QLD and SA maintained lower prices due to their own generation capacity. The situation reflects broader changes in energy consumption and supply, partially driven by reduced solar output in late afternoon.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as observed on the day reported. Its root_cause is: Hot weather increasing electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-12-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and the National Electricity Market (NEM) at large. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and suppliers in NSW and other regions within the NEM. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand; transmission congestion affecting price discrepancies across regions; reduced solar generation later in the day contributing to demand shifts.

title: NSW demand currently forecast to peak up at 13,500MW on Tuesday 19th December
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW demand currently forecast to peak up at 13,500MW on Tuesday 19th December" posted at 18-12-2017 07:46:00 AM is: The demand forecast for New South Wales (NSW) is expected to reach 13,500MW on Tuesday, 19th December at 16:30 NEM time, due to increasingly hot weather conditions. This anticipated demand will be significant, being only 1,000MW below the all-time record, despite previous demand destruction such as the closure of the Kurri Kurri aluminium smelter. The demand scenario is contextualized with historical peaks, suggesting potential challenges for the grid; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Tuesday, 19th December 2017. Its root_cause is: Increasingly hot weather forecast. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 18-12-2017 07:46:00 AM. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Electricity grid operators and large electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes is: Increasingly hot weather, forecast demand peak.

title: Coincident warm demand forecast for VIC and NSW tomorrow late afternoon (Monday 18th December)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Coincident warm demand forecast for VIC and NSW tomorrow late afternoon (Monday 18th December)" posted at 17-12-2017 08:35:00 PM is: A high electricity demand is forecasted for Victoria and New South Wales for the late afternoon on Monday, December 18th, due to a warm weather pattern. The demand is especially significant given the decrease in available generation capacity following the closure of the Hazelwood power station in April 2017. The National Electricity Market is expected to reach 32,023MW, with peak demands in specific states predicted at 11,933MW for NSW and 8,533MW for VIC. South Australia is also expected to experience increased demand, with the Australian Energy Market Operator issuing LOR1 low reserve notices for VIC and SA, with additional notice for NSW on Tuesday. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (1-2 days). Its root_cause is: Coincident high electricity demand due to warm weather and reduced generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Low. Its dates are: 18-12-2017 for the expected demand peak, with reserve notice on 19-12-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria, New South Wales, South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM states. Its cause type is: Weather and generation capacity-related. Its causes are: High demand forecast due to warm weather, Hazelwood power station closure leading to reduced generation capacity, repair outages, and peak demand exceeding previous records.

title: “Scheduled Demand” in Victoria stopped short of 8,500MW today (lower than forecast) but the demand peak forecast for NSW tomorrow has grown to almost 13,000MW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Scheduled Demand” in Victoria stopped short of 8,500MW today (lower than forecast) but the demand peak forecast for NSW tomorrow has grown to almost 13,000MW" posted at 13-12-2017 06:37:00 PM is: The news article discusses electricity demand forecasts for Victoria and New South Wales (NSW) in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the summer of 2017-18. Actual electricity demand in Victoria did not reach the anticipated 9,000MW, peaking instead at 8,455MW. Consequently, the electricity price remained below $150/MWh. In contrast, demand forecasts for NSW for the following day climbed to nearly 13,000MW, reflecting a 1,000MW increase from earlier predictions. Potential price increases were considered, though similar forecasts earlier in the day did not materialize. The article also highlights the challenges and unpredictability inherent in demand forecasting. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, within a day. Its root cause is: Forecasting variability and changing demand predictions. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 13-12-2017, with forecasts for 14-12-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market operators. Its cause type is: Forecasting discrepancy. Its causes are: Lower than anticipated demand in Victoria and higher revised forecasts for NSW.

title: Hornsdale Power Reserve continues to reach new milestones with a discharge rate of 100 MW today
author: Carol Hunter
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hornsdale Power Reserve continues to reach new milestones with a discharge rate of 100 MW today" posted at 13-12-2017 03:52:00 PM is: The Hornsdale Power Reserve has achieved a significant milestone by reaching a discharge rate of 100 MW between 11:20 – 11:25 NEM time, accompanied by the longest continuous charging period from 02:55 – 05:50 NEM time. These activities were tracked using NEMreview v7 and are accessible via specific links for users with appropriate licenses. Real-time data was also available through the NEM-Watch widget sponsored by RenewEconomy. The news highlights the increasing capabilities and operational milestones of the Hornsdale Power Reserve battery system.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing, given the nature of battery storage in the grid. Its root_cause is: Testing and operation of the Hornsdale Power Reserve battery. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-12-2017. Its affected region is: The region covered by the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Users and stakeholders monitoring grid stability and energy storage capabilities. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Discharge rate testing, continuous charging, monitoring through NEMreview and NEM-Watch.

title: High temperatures to give demand a kick in Victoria tomorrow (Wednesday 13th December)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "High temperatures to give demand a kick in Victoria tomorrow (Wednesday 13th December)" posted at 12-12-2017 03:44:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) anticipates that high temperatures projected for South Australia and Victoria on December 13, 2017, will increase electricity demand within these regions. The forecasted heatwave presents an opportunity to observe the Tesla/Neoen Hornsdale Power Reserve, notably the world's largest battery, as it may play a significant role in balancing the grid during high demand. The event also offers a chance to monitor how the battery performs under these conditions via various platforms including NEMwatch and RenewEconomy. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term (immediate to 1-2 days). Its root cause is: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 12-12-2017 to 13-12-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Consumers and grid operators in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: High temperatures forecasted to increase electricity demand, potential involvement of the Hornsdale Power Reserve battery system.

title: Electricity demand set to climb in NSW on Thursday with the hot weather forecast
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Electricity demand set to climb in NSW on Thursday with the hot weather forecast" posted at 11-12-2017 08:59:00 PM is: The Bureau of Meteorology is predicting extreme temperatures above 40 degrees Celsius in Western Sydney, notably Penrith, resulting in a significant climb in electricity demand for New South Wales on Thursday, according to AEMO’s forecasts. Despite the rise, demand is still expected to remain 2,000MW below the all-time maximum. Large-scale solar and wind power outputs are projected to peak on the same day, while surplus generation levels are forecast to drop below zero temporarily. This will prompt NSW to depend heavily on interconnections with Queensland and Victoria, potentially causing high electricity prices due to supply scarcity. It's important to monitor demand spikes in Victoria and South Australia, which are also rising as per recent data. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short term, focused on Thursday. Its root cause is: Extreme temperatures leading to increased demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-12-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: High temperature forecasts, increased electricity demand, reliance on inter-state power interconnections.

title: AEMO updates generator combinations for SA system strength
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO updates generator combinations for SA system strength" posted at 08-12-2017 12:50:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has published an update on generator combinations to meet system strength requirements in South Australia. As of December 8, 2017, AEMO has identified three new generator combinations that fulfill the minimum system strength requirements. The update increases the limit on non-synchronous generation from 1200 MW to 1295 MW and synchronous generation from 1700 MW to 1870 MW for Heywood (Vic to SA) transfers. This change is part of AEMO’s ongoing investigation into system strength in South Australia, with further updates expected by late January 2018. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium-term impact expected, with further updates by late January 2018. Its root_cause is: Investigation and adjustment of system strength requirements and constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-12-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and regulatory update. Its causes are: Identification of new generator combinations, adjustment of system strength limits, and continuous investigation by AEMO.

title: Big ramp in QLD demand Thursday evening to be first time above 8000MW this summer
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Big ramp in QLD demand Thursday evening to be first time above 8000MW this summer" posted at 08-12-2017 07:33:00 AM is: The article highlights a significant increase in electricity demand in Queensland, which surpassed 8,000MW for the first time during the summer season, marking a notable peak since late October. Despite a drop in temperatures earlier in the day, demand continued to rise, indicating a trend of high electricity demand likely to continue throughout summer, with potential peaks reaching up to 9,500MW. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and likely to continue throughout summer. Its root_cause is: Increased electricity demand in Queensland during the summer season. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-12-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market Demand. Its causes are: Significant ramp in electricity demand, despite a drop in temperatures earlier in the day.

title: How does frequency regulation help avoid Blackouts?
author: Bruce Miller
classified_content: The summary of the news "How does frequency regulation help avoid Blackouts?" posted at 05-12-2017 12:51:00 PM is: The article delves into the significance of frequency regulation in preventing power system blackouts, taking cues from the South Australia blackout incident. It highlights the systemic flaws in the regulation Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) markets, where units within South Australia were not participating, causing an over-reliance on external regions to manage frequency. This absence of participation and inappropriate dispatch strategies led to insufficient headroom on the Heywood Interconnector, making the system vulnerable to failure. The article urges the division of regulation FCAS between regions and the introduction of tie line control at weak system points to improve grid stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate during the blackout period with potential ongoing impacts on market design. Its root_cause is: Insufficient regional participation in regulation FCAS and inappropriate dispatch strategies. Its accident scale is: Statewide blackout in South Australia. Its dates are: 05-12-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: General populace and industries dependent on reliable power supply. Its cause type is: Systemic regulation and dispatch market design issues. Its causes are: Non-participation of South Australian units in regulation FCAS, over-reliance on inter-region frequency management, inadequate interconnector headroom, and wide frequency range tolerances.

title: The first stage of the Kidston Energy Park powers up
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The first stage of the Kidston Energy Park powers up" posted at 04-12-2017 10:35:00 AM is: The first stage of Genex Power's Kidston Energy Park in northern Queensland began generating electricity, contributing to the National Electricity Market (NEM) with an initial 50MW PV array. As the largest solar power plant in Queensland at the time, this development marks a significant step forward in renewable energy. The project is set to expand further by 2021 with an additional 270MW of solar PV and a 2000MWh pumped hydro energy storage capacity, highlighting ongoing developments in renewable energy infrastructure within the region. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term impact starting from December 2017. Its root_cause is: Expansion of renewable energy capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-12-2017. Its affected region is: Northern Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Planned infrastructure development. Its causes are: Introduction of 50MW solar PV array and future expansion plans including additional solar capacity and pumped hydro storage.

title: Unfortunately no time for a “Best Peak Demand Forecaster” competition for summer 2017-18
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Who's the best demand forecaster in the NEM?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unfortunately no time for a “Best Peak Demand Forecaster” competition for summer 2017-18" posted at 03-12-2017 03:57:00 PM is: The article discusses the inability to host the "Best Peak Demand Forecaster" competition for the summer of 2017-18 due to time constraints amidst the critical period of energy transition and demand changes in the electricity market. The news highlights the evolving nature of demand measurement and infrastructure development in the National Electricity Market (NEM), which requires a new focus if the competition is revived in the future. To aid understanding of demand trends, the company has developed free widgets enabling public access to energy consumption data influenced by climate. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Focus on energy transition and complexity of demand forecasting. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-12-2017. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational change. Its causes are: Shift in demand dynamics, focus on energy transition, time constraints for competition.

title: Hornsdale Power Reserve (a.k.a. “the worlds biggest battery”) supplied SA for over an hour yesterday, peaking at 71 MW discharge
author: Carol Hunter
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hornsdale Power Reserve (a.k.a. “the worlds biggest battery”) supplied SA for over an hour yesterday, peaking at 71 MW discharge" posted at 01-12-2017 04:30:00 PM is: The Hornsdale Power Reserve, known as the world's largest battery, effectively supplied South Australia by discharging at a peak rate of 71 MW for over an hour on November 30, 2017. This action was part of the testing phase leading up to its official operation starting December 1, 2017; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Testing phase of a new large-scale battery. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: November 30, 2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational event. Its causes are: Testing discharge of the Hornsdale Power Reserve, discharge at a peak of 71 MW.

title: Not quite summer and volatility returns to Victoria and South Australia
author: Carol Hunter
classified_content: The summary of the news "Not quite summer and volatility returns to Victoria and South Australia" posted at 01-12-2017 01:03:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) reported challenges in electricity reserves for Victoria and South Australia due to higher temperatures and a line trip caused by lightning, leading to an Actual Lack of Reserve (LOR1) declaration in Victoria. This event, combined with limited interconnector flow, resulted in increased electricity prices in both regions during the late afternoon of November 29th, 2017. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate but potentially ongoing throughout summer. Its root_cause is: Lightning-induced line trips and high temperatures. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 01-12-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Victoria and South Australia. Its cause type is: Natural and operational disturbances. Its causes are: Lightning strikes leading to line trips, high temperatures, and limited interconnector flow.

title: AEMC released Final Determination on Five Minute Settlement on 28th November 2017
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC released Final Determination on Five Minute Settlement on 28th November 2017" posted at 28-11-2017 06:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) released a Final Determination on the implementation of Five Minute Settlement in the National Electricity Market (NEM), which is a significant rule change aimed at aligning trading intervals to five minutes to improve market efficiency. This decision marks a transitional period set to be fully implemented by 1 July 2021 but was later delayed to 1 October 2021. The change necessitates updated metering infrastructure and information technology systems for NEM participants, with various new and existing meters required to record five-minute data. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: A regulatory decision to improve market alignment and efficiency. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-11-2017 (announcement), 01-07-2021 (initial implementation date), 01-10-2021 (actual implementation date). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: NEM participants, Market Generators, Small Generation Aggregators. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes are: Implementation of Five Minute Settlement rule, alignment of trading intervals to five minutes, requirement for updated metering infrastructure and IT systems.

title: It’s complete baloney to claim that there’s “less than 1%” of peak demand in the NEM that is currently providing Demand Response
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "It’s complete baloney to claim that there’s “less than 1%” of peak demand in the NEM that is currently providing Demand Response" posted at 25-11-2017 11:26:00 PM is: The article criticizes a misleading claim in EcoGeneration that "less than 1%" of peak demand in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia engages in demand response. The writer disputes this, referring to various sources including the Australia Institute and the Oakley Greenwood report, revealing disconnections and errors in the reported figures. They argue that the figure stems from a limited survey, not reflecting the actual broader scope of demand response in the NEM. The underlying causes of such understatements are also explored, including market differences and commercial reticence. This misrepresentation could potentially impact perceptions of the electricity market's stability and effectiveness. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing until misconceptions are addressed. Its root cause is: Misreporting and misunderstanding of data regarding demand response. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-11-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and policymakers relying on accurate demand response data. Its cause type is: Data misinterpretation and reporting errors. Its causes are: Sloppy journalism, misunderstanding of demand response versus demand management, and reliance on limited survey data.

title: If you increase your consumption of energy at a time when average emissions intensity is low, you’re not necessarily doing a good thing for the environment
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "If you increase your consumption of energy at a time when average emissions intensity is low, you’re not necessarily doing a good thing for the environment" posted at 25-11-2017 05:23:00 PM is: The article discusses the importance of focusing on the emissions intensity of marginal generation rather than average emissions when deciding the timing of energy consumption. It highlights the common misconception of relying on average metrics, emphasizing that decisions should be based on the emissions intensity of the additional electricity that would be consumed. The piece critiques Oliver Corradi's suggestions in the CITIES project, which advocates for adjusting consumption based on average emissions intensity, and argues for a more nuanced approach to reducing emissions by focusing on marginal generation. The article also underlines the significance of improving energy literacy to tackle the challenges of energy transition effectively; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Misconceptions in energy consumption metrics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 25-11-2017. Its affected region is: Global (general context of energy consumption and emissions). Its affected key users is: General consumers and energy policymakers. Its cause type is: Conceptual misunderstanding. Its causes is: Misinterpretations of average versus marginal emissions intensity, focus on average emissions rather than marginal emissions.

title: Hornsdale Power Reserve (a.k.a. “the world’s biggest battery”) begins to charge, and also discharges for the first time on Saturday
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hornsdale Power Reserve (a.k.a. “the world’s biggest battery”) begins to charge, and also discharges for the first time on Saturday" posted at 25-11-2017 12:29:00 PM is: On November 25, 2017, the Hornsdale Power Reserve, known as the world's largest battery and operated by Tesla and Neoen, began its first significant charging and discharging operations. This event marked an important milestone as the battery charged at a rate of about 30MW and discharged for just over an hour at a similar rate later in the day. This development comes shortly before the end of its 100-day construction deadline, attracting attention from the energy sector. Visualization updates for monitoring the battery's activity are being shared with stakeholders.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and potentially ongoing as the battery is now operational. Its root cause is: The commissioning and testing phase of the Hornsdale Power Reserve. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-11-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Commissioning/testing of new infrastructure. Its causes are: Commissioning and operational testing of the battery system.

title: Some simple calculations to help get my head around the scale of the numbers pertaining to Battery Storage
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some simple calculations to help get my head around the scale of the numbers pertaining to Battery Storage" posted at 24-11-2017 12:45:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenges of scaling battery storage to manage daily variability in solar energy production in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM). The author uses hypothetical calculations to estimate that approximately 67,728 MWh of storage would be needed to balance daily solar output fluctuations, which would require the equivalent of three Tesla Powerwall 2 batteries per household for over 1.5 million installations. The article emphasizes the absence of real historical data on large-scale battery storage, underscoring the need for careful planning in the energy transition. It also highlights the significance of upcoming projects like the Hornsdale Power Reserve in informing future storage strategies.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term as it pertains to future energy transition strategies. Its root_cause is: The need to integrate battery storage to manage solar energy production variability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 24-11-2017. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Potentially residential solar PV users. Its cause type is: Hypothetical scenario estimation. Its causes is: Challenges in estimating required battery storage capacity to manage solar output variability, lack of historical data guiding current storage initiatives, and ongoing energy transition efforts.

title: Following up, with a top-down (NEM-wide) look at daily solar production (small and large) through 2017
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Following up, with a top-down (NEM-wide) look at daily solar production (small and large) through 2017" posted at 20-11-2017 07:07:00 AM is: The article explores the variability and trends in solar power production across the National Electricity Market (NEM) throughout 2017, with a focus on the impact of weather conditions, seasonal variations, and systemic challenges resulting from increased solar PV penetration. It notes significant fluctuations in daily output and emphasizes the need for strategic approaches to address these challenges, such as improving the visibility and measurement of small-scale solar contributions, leveraging geographical diversity, demand response, battery storage, and revisiting the "baseload" concept. The analysis covers an extended time period and presents insights into the implications for both micro-level (residential) and macro-level (grid-wide) solar production dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Root cause is: Variability in solar production due to weather and growth in solar penetration. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: 1st January 2017 to end of 2017, with specific notes on low output days on 19th May 2017 and several days in October 2017. Affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Affected key users are: Energy consumers and industry stakeholders managing energy supply and demand. Cause type is: Natural and systemic. Causes are: Seasonal variations, weather conditions, increases in solar PV installations, and systemic challenges in energy supply and grid management.

title: What’s the market data saying about this Summer?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s the market data saying about this Summer?" posted at 16-11-2017 11:33:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges facing the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia during the summer of 2017-18, highlighting concerns about a tight supply-demand balance. It follows multiple contributing factors, including the retirement of Hazelwood, load shedding incidents in South Australia and New South Wales, and government initiatives like the SA Energy Plan. The report uses NEM data to analyze past and forward-looking price indicators such as ASX-traded cap futures, identifying a pattern of rising price volatility expectations for Q1 2018 in Victoria and South Australia, driven by potential system demands and shortfalls in system reserves through AEMO's PASA assessments. These reinforce concerns about electricity supply adequacy under extreme conditions as summer approaches. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Over the summer months of 2017-18. Its root_cause is: Anticipated demand peaks and potential supply shortfalls, compounded by past load shedding events and retiring generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-11-2017. Its affected region is: Southern NEM states, notably South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and industries dependent on stable electricity supply in affected regions. Its cause type is: Market and supply-demand balance issues. Its causes are: Tight supply-demand balance, historical load shedding, generator retirement (Hazelwood), high expectations of price volatility in summer 2017-18, system reserve shortfalls indicated by PASA assessments.

title: Some initial musings about my own Rooftop Solar Learning Exercise
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Personal experiences with Distributed Energy Resources
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some initial musings about my own Rooftop Solar Learning Exercise" posted at 07-11-2017 01:34:00 PM is: The article discusses personal experiences and observations from the installation and use of residential solar PV systems, particularly focusing on data management and the challenges associated with distributed energy resources. It underscores the importance of data integrity in the electricity sector and highlights the diverse motivations and experiences of solar users. The narrative also reflects on various stakeholders involved in solar energy management and operational complexities, data privacy concerns, and the potential unintended consequences of increased rooftop solar adoption. Key takeaways include the need for tailored energy solutions, consideration of customer diversity, and an emphasis on energy literacy. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Transition to renewable energy and adoption of solar PV. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-11-2017 01:34:00 PM. Its affected region is: Brisbane, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential solar PV owners/users. Its cause type is: Technological/Operational. Its causes are: Increasing adoption of rooftop solar PV, data management issues, and operational challenges with distributed energy systems.

title: Analytical Challenge – avoiding Recency Bias
author: Paul McArdle
topic: A collection of some core analytical challenges
classified_content: The summary of the news "Analytical Challenge – avoiding Recency Bias" posted at 07-11-2017 01:26:00 PM is: The article discusses the tendency to overemphasize recent data while analyzing residential solar PV output, thereby neglecting the broader historical perspective essential for understanding performance changes over time; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Overemphasis on recent data. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-11-2017. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Analytical error. Its causes are: Recency bias in data analysis.

title: The two, SA Government-owned, “Emergency Generators” produce first power whilst commissioning
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The two, SA Government-owned, “Emergency Generators” produce first power whilst commissioning" posted at 27-10-2017 03:46:00 PM is: The two South Australian Government-owned emergency generators have begun producing their first power as part of the commissioning process. These generators are expected to operate infrequently once the commissioning phase is completed; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term during the commissioning phase. Its root_cause is: Integration of emergency backup power infrastructure into the grid. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-10-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Infrastructure commissioning. Its causes are: Commissioning of new government-owned emergency power generators.

title: No Guarantee of Success
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "No Guarantee of Success" posted at 27-10-2017 09:58:00 AM is: The article critiques the proposed National Energy Guarantee (NEG) framework, arguing it represents a significant but hastily introduced shift in the National Electricity Market (NEM) without thorough consultation or detail. It outlines concerns about the NEG's Reliability Guarantee, which shifts investment decision-making from market-driven to centrally-planned targets, potentially misapplying the concept of "dispatchability" and oversimplifying contract markets. The article suggests this could lead to inefficiencies, increased costs, and reduced competitiveness, particularly impacting smaller players compared to larger integrated gentailers. The author emphasizes the need for further consultation and development to address these concerns effectively.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Introduction of the National Energy Guarantee framework. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-10-2017 09:58:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Policy change. Its causes are: Proposed changes to the National Electricity Market, introduction of the NEG's Reliability Guarantee, shift to centrally-planned capacity targets, misapplication of dispatchability, simplification of contract markets.

title: Possibilities of Peak Demand (VIC and SA) during summer 2017-18
author: Rob Davis
classified_content: The summary of the news "Possibilities of Peak Demand (VIC and SA) during summer 2017-18" posted at 25-10-2017 02:44:00 PM is: The article discusses the potential implications of a La Nina event forecasted for the summer of 2017-18, specifically regarding its impact on electricity demand in Victoria (VIC) and South Australia (SA). While La Nina conditions may suppress extreme temperatures on Australia's east coast cities, southern regions such as Adelaide and Melbourne are expected to experience significant heat waves. The prediction uses Cooling Degree Days (CDD) as an indicator, highlighting that Melbourne might see a rise in peak electricity demand, potentially reaching 1100-1200 MW above recent summer peaks. In Adelaide, a possible increase of 250 MW during peak events is anticipated. This increased demand could be exacerbated by combined heat impacts due to La Nina, alongside concerns of reduced wind power generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Summer 2017-18. Its root cause is: The forecasted La Nina event, along with the warming trend, is expected to influence extreme heat distributions in VIC and SA impacting peak electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-10-2017 (and the anticipated impact over summer 2017-18). Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC) and South Australia (SA). Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Meteorological events and climatic trends. Its causes are: La Nina event likely suppressing temperatures in some areas while potentially causing extreme heat waves in others, historical climatology showing warming trends, and certain regional events (such as high-pressure systems) exacerbating temperatures.

title: Villain #2 – we, the voting public
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Villain #2 – we, the voting public," posted at 11-10-2017 06:33:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges of establishing a stable energy policy amidst Australia's energy transition, highlighting the role of the voting public as "Villain #2" for contributing to the instability. The author reflects on the lack of bipartisan consensus on energy policy and the influence of public opinion and debate in shaping political inaction. Despite significant discourse on climate change, public division persists, affecting the ability to craft durable policy solutions. Consequently, this uncertainty contributes to an unstable energy framework, complicating the transition process. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Public division and lack of consensus on energy policies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-10-2017. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Socio-political. Its causes are: Public opinion division, lack of political consensus on energy policies, ineffective policymaking.

title: Villain #1 – our Emperors (present, past and would-be) have no clothes
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Villain #1 – our Emperors (present, past and would-be) have no clothes" posted at 09-10-2017 09:54:00 AM is: The article highlights the persistent failures in Australian energy and climate policy over the past decade, exacerbated by political indecision and partisan politics. It underscores the energy crisis triggered by the South Australian blackout on 28th September 2016 and notes various opinions from industry figures and politicians who blame policy failures for rising energy costs and instability. The root cause is identified as a lack of coherent national policy and the ongoing use of energy issues for political leverage, leading to a lack of investment certainty and heightened market volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, with ongoing impacts as policy indecision continues. Its root_cause is: Policy failures and political indecision. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28th September 2016 (South Australian blackout), multiple dates including 23rd August, 1st September, 5th September, 6th September, 7th September, 8th September, 12th September, 14th September, 30th September, 6th October, 8th October. Its affected region is: Australia, particularly South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers, corporate Australia. Its cause type is: Systemic policy failure. Its causes are: Political indecision, policy vacuum, lack of investment certainty.

title: A long list of Villains – that have each contributed to our “energy crisis”
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A long list of Villains – that have each contributed to our 'energy crisis'" posted at 09-10-2017 09:32:00 AM is: The Australian domestic energy sector is currently facing a crisis, which has not developed overnight but instead is the result of a combination of factors over time. The article highlights that the crisis involves various 'villains,' including political inefficacy across all parties, lack of energy literacy, and inadequate problem-solving approaches, with roots going back over a decade. The ongoing debate over energy transition policies, market rules, and vested interests among stakeholders are significant contributors. The article suggests that without identifying and addressing these root causes, long-term solutions will be difficult to implement. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Long-term. Its root cause is: Inefficient political leadership and mixed policies on energy transition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-10-2017 09:32:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: General Australian energy users. Its cause type is: Policy and governance issues. Its causes are: Political inefficacy, lack of integrated energy policies, and ineffective problem-solving approaches concerning energy transition.

title: The solar component of the Gullen Range Power Station started operation on 14th September
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The solar component of the Gullen Range Power Station started operation on 14th September" posted at 05-10-2017 02:49:00 PM is: (The article discusses the initiation of the 10MW solar component at the Gullen Range Power Station on 14th September. This addition augments the existing wind-powered output and illustrates differences in output cycles between wind and solar energy at the site. It's part of a broader energy transition including an increasing number of renewable developments.); Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Renewable energy expansion. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-09-2017. Its affected region is: Gullen Range, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Planned infrastructure development. Its causes are: Initiation of the solar component at the power station.

title: Here’s two odd examples being used in attempt to justify a claim of why privatisation will lead to higher spot prices
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Here’s two odd examples being used in attempt to justify a claim of why privatisation will lead to higher spot prices" posted at 03-10-2017 02:10:00 PM is: The article critiques the argument that public ownership of energy assets may be preferable to privatisation, especially for state-owned entities providing essential services or holding monopolistic positions. It highlights two dubious examples: firstly, that government retention of MacGen could have made the situation around the Liddell Power Station less challenging, and secondly, a government directive to change bidding practices in Queensland, which momentarily reduced wholesale electricity prices. However, these instances are questioned for their suitability in arguing for government ownership, considering previous decisions like the merger of Queensland GOCs, which increased market concentration and control over prices. The piece also cautions against conflating the questions of market existence and asset ownership. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: episodic, with specific short-term impacts related to governmental directives and market practices. Its root cause is: government decisions regarding asset ownership and market structure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-06-2017 related to initial market impact, and historical references to the 2011 merger. Its affected region is: Queensland for the market impact example and New South Wales for the Liddell discussion. Its affected key users are: energy consumers in Queensland and potentially New South Wales depending on hypothetical scenarios. Its cause type is: Policy-related decisions. Its causes are: government directives on market participation, historical asset mergers, and privatisation considerations.

title: A selected (and evolving) chronology of the QLD Government’s involvement in the QLD Generation sector
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A selected (and evolving) chronology of the QLD Government’s involvement in the QLD Generation sector" posted at 03-10-2017 12:56:00 PM is: The article provides a detailed chronology of the Queensland Government's engagement in the electricity generation sector over the decades, highlighting significant events, policy decisions, and market outcomes, particularly from 1994 to early 2017. It discusses the impact of these governmental actions on the National Electricity Market (NEM), such as interconnected energy infrastructure, and the influence of major political decisions like the carbon tax and proposed generator mergers on electricity prices and market volatility. It also examines the implications of the Queensland Government's policies and actions on electricity pricing and market structure over time. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Government involvement and policy decisions in the energy market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-10-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and strategic decisions. Its causes are: Government sales and acquisitions of power stations, implementation of carbon tax, market mergers and acquisitions, market volatility due to extreme climatic conditions.

title: Comparison between Liddell Power Station (in NSW) with Muja A&B Power Station (in WA)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Comparison between Liddell Power Station (in NSW) with Muja A&B Power Station (in WA)" posted at 03-10-2017 10:40:00 AM is: The article examines the comparisons made between Liddell Power Station in New South Wales and Muja A&B Power Station in Western Australia, highlighting ongoing debates about the potential closures of these aging facilities. Both power stations have faced technical issues, cost overruns, and project delays due to their age, with discussions about government intervention to keep them operational. Notably, Muja A&B's refurbishment faced significant budget blowouts and is scheduled for closure, while Liddell's future remains uncertain with potential government purchase under consideration. The article explores data trends in output and utilization at both stations, with Liddell showing steady utilization until 2016 and Muja's smaller units being more sporadically operational.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term potential impact due to energy policy decisions. Its root_cause is: Aging infrastructure and economic considerations in maintaining older power stations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-10-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales (Liddell) and Western Australia (Muja). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Infrastructure and policy challenges. Its causes are: Technical issues with aging plants, higher cost impacts, government intervention considerations, and project delays.

title: Is the market, that wasn’t broken, now dead?
author: Andrew Bonwick
classified_content: The summary of the news "Is the market, that wasn’t broken, now dead?" posted at 28-09-2017 03:18:00 PM is: The article discusses the transformation of the Australian energy market from government-owned monopolies to privatized entities and the impact of government subsidies on energy production. It highlights the issues stemming from the mismatch between energy production and demand, particularly with wind and solar energy. The article also touches on the political climate affecting energy policies and the consequences of anti-gas drilling policies, leading to increased domestic gas prices. With significant subsidies, renewable energies have resulted in zero-priced energy at certain times, altering market pricing dynamics. These changes, alongside direct government investments and regulations, have introduced uncertainties and high prices for industrial and domestic users. Furthermore, government actions, including asset building and regulated prices, are critiqued as potentially detrimental in the long run; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Over the next 3-5 years. Its root_cause is: Government subsidies, anti-gas drilling policies, and energy market transformation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 28-09-2017. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Industrial and domestic energy consumers. Its cause type is: Policy and market dynamics. Its causes is: Government privatization, subsidies shifting production focus, anti-gas drilling policy impact, direct investment and regulation.

title: Using this train analogy to critique some recent developments with Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS)
author: Bruce Miller
classified_content: The summary of the news "Using this train analogy to critique some recent developments with Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS)" posted at 28-09-2017 12:13:00 PM is: The article critiques the market-based approach to Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) in the National Electricity Market (NEM) using a train analogy, where various engines represent power generators. It suggests that this system, which determines frequency control via economic bidding rather than technical needs, may compromise system reliability and security by introducing unpredictability and potential failures in frequency control. The author argues for a shift to lighter inertia systems and better automated controls to improve power system stability, citing the South Australia blackout as an example of the risks posed by the current approach.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant); Its category is: Grid frequency issues; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term impact due to the potential for compromised frequency control; Its root_cause is: Implementation of a market-based mechanism for frequency control rather than technical assessment; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 28-09-2017; Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia), with a specific reference to South Australia; Its affected key users are: Energy providers and consumers within the NEM, with emphasis on technical and engineering stakeholders; Its cause type is: Systemic and regulatory; Its causes are: Market-based FCAS implementation, lack of adequate automated control, and insufficient system inertia management.

title: Introducing a train analogy to explain Power Systems 101
author: Bruce Miller
classified_content: The summary of the news "Introducing a train analogy to explain Power Systems 101" posted at 28-09-2017 12:05:00 PM is: The article explores how analogies can be effectively used to communicate complex power system concepts. Using the analogy of a long train with engines and carriages, the article explains various technical aspects of power systems such as generators, loads, system inertia, and ancillary services. It highlights the challenges of transitioning to renewable energy sources and the importance of understanding system stability and frequency control. While the analogy simplifies these concepts for better public comprehension, it acknowledges that experts might find the simplifications lacking in depth. The piece emphasizes the need for detailed study and STEM proficiency to fully grasp the intricacies of electrical power systems; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Explanation of power system concepts through analogy to improve public understanding. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-09-2017. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Non-specialists and policy makers. Its cause type is: Conceptual explanation. Its causes are: Use of analogies, transition to renewable energy, need for ancillary services understanding.

title: September Update: More Regulation on the Retail Side
author: Connor James
classified_content: The summary of the news "September Update: More Regulation on the Retail Side" posted at 27-09-2017 02:13:00 PM is: The article discusses the increased attention and proposed regulations in the energy retail and embedded networks sector in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in September 2017. There is continued concern about pricing and a growing number of applications for retail authorisations, with new players like Discover Energy and ZEN Energy entering the market. Discover Energy focuses on solar offers, while ZEN Energy is targeting large energy consumers. The rise of embedded networks by non-traditional players such as building developers has presented regulatory challenges. The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) draft report on September 12 recommended changes to the regulatory framework to better protect customers and improve the regulatory regime. The final report is due on November 28, 2017. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: New applications for energy retail authorisations and interest in embedded networks. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12 September 2017 (AEMC draft report published), 18 October 2017 (ZEN Energy submissions due), 20 October 2017 (Discover Energy submissions due), 28 November 2017 (final AEMC report due). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Embedded network customers. Its cause type is: Regulatory challenges and market adjustments. Its causes are: Growing number of retail authorisation applications, regulatory gaps in embedded networks, need for consumer protection improvements.

title: Initial questions about the LGC market
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Initial questions about the LGC market" posted at 26-09-2017 05:16:00 PM is: Since late 2016, NEMreview has been developing its version 7, which involves moving the platform online to facilitate data set additions. This BETA version now includes LGC price data from Green Energy Trading and registration data from the Clean Energy Regulator. However, users are cautioned to expect potential errors, and feedback is encouraged to improve accuracy. The new capabilities are intended to generate insights into the energy market despite the current development stage.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Development of software to include LGC price and registration data. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 26-09-2017 05:16:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: NEMreview clients and those interested in energy market trends. Its cause type is: Data integration and software development. Its causes are: Moving NEMreview online, incorporating LGC price data, and potential data errors in the BETA version.

title: Why is wind production being curtailed (or “dispatched down”, more strictly speaking)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why is wind production being curtailed (or “dispatched down”, more strictly speaking)?" posted at 17-09-2017 01:55:00 PM is: The article discusses the curtailment of wind energy production in South Australia, noting over 65,746 MWh curtailed, equating to 6% of capacity. This situation arises when supply exceeds demand or when network conditions require it, with a specific constraint equation “S_WIND_1200_AUTO” used by AEMO after the 2016 blackout. These constraints are likely to increase as more projects develop in the region. This curtailment affects the economics of Semi-Scheduled projects, altering revenue and necessitating adaptation to market complexities. The report highlights the potential end of a 'grace period' for new generators and indicates that the economic impact might not be wholly negative, as it might prevent extremely low pricing situations. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Likely to increase in the future with new project developments. Its root cause is: Network conditions and supply-demand imbalance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-09-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Wind energy producers. Its cause type is: System operational constraints and policy impacts. Its causes are: Supply exceeds demand scenarios; network condition constraints by AEMO; increased complexity in market participation for generators.

title: Analytical Challenge – determining “what might have otherwise been?”
author: Paul McArdle
topic: A collection of some core analytical challenges
classified_content: The summary of the news "Analytical Challenge – determining “what might have otherwise been?”" posted at 15-09-2017 03:55:00 PM is: The article discusses analytical challenges faced in understanding historical events' impacts on the electricity market, with particular focus on the diverse interpretations of data and the role of hypothetical scenarios in shaping market analysis. It mentions a specific instance where claims linked the establishment of the market to increased wholesale prices, suggesting that these claims may be based on a flawed premise. It also highlights attempts to estimate energy curtailment due to specific grid constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Allegations linking market establishment to current price increases and interpretation issues in analysis. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-09-2017. Its affected region is: Likely the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Analytical misinterpretation. Its causes are: Claims about historical market decisions affecting current prices, constraints affecting grid management.

title: How much wind-powered electricity production has been curtailed in SA since these new constraints were invoked?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How much wind-powered electricity production has been curtailed in SA since these new constraints were invoked?" posted at 15-09-2017 01:32:00 PM is: The article discusses the curtailment of wind-powered electricity production in South Australia due to new constraints imposed by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO). It highlights that these constraints result in the reduction of wind energy output and drive down electricity prices to the Market Price Floor. The curtailment has occurred numerous times since July 19, 2017, with a substantial estimated 65,746 MWh of potential wind energy curtailed over an 8-week period, despite high projections for wind resource yields. This reduction in production negatively impacts wind farm revenues, as it was not accounted for in their original business models. The root of this issue lies in AEMO's control and curtailment practices necessary to manage grid stability amid high wind generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing since July 19, 2017, with the impact noted over an 8-week period up to September 13, 2017. Its root cause is: AEMO's imposition of constraints to manage grid stability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: July 19, 2017, to September 13, 2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Wind farm operators. Its cause type is: Operational/Regulatory. Its causes are: Wind generation curtailment due to AEMO constraints; grid management requirements to maintain system stability.

title: How significant would it be, if Mt Piper were forced to close (at least temporarily)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How significant would it be, if Mt Piper were forced to close (at least temporarily)?" posted at 13-09-2017 10:32:00 AM is: The article discusses the potential impacts of a temporary closure of the Mt Piper coal-fired station in New South Wales (NSW), due to a NSW Court of Appeal decision affecting the planning consent of the Springvale coal mine. The mine is the sole supplier of coal to Mt Piper, which provides approximately 13% of the "Scheduled Energy" consumed by customers in NSW. This situation poses a risk to fuel supply, highlighting the dependency on coal and raising concerns about energy stability in the state; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to unknown, depending on legal proceedings and alternative arrangements. Its root_cause is: Legal decision affecting the planning consent of the coal mine supplying fuel to Mt Piper. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-09-2017 10:32:00 AM. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. The affected key users are: Energy consumers in NSW, particularly those dependent on coal-generated electricity. Its cause type is: Legal and regulatory. Its causes are: NSW Court of Appeal decision on Springvale coal mine affecting coal supply to Mt Piper station.

title: Decoding AEMO’s latest ESOO–will the lights stay on this summer?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Decoding AEMO’s latest ESOO–will the lights stay on this summer?" posted at 07-09-2017 03:57:00 PM is: The article details the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) and its projections for reliability across the National Electricity Market (NEM) for the 2017-18 summer. AEMO indicates the potential for Unserved Energy (USE) in South Australia and Victoria could approach or exceed the reliability standard due to demand growth and power stations retiring. Despite new solar and wind investments, concerns remain for energy reliability, especially with the retirement of key power stations like Liddell. Measures like the addition of battery capacity, diesel generation leasing, and demand-side responses aim to offset risks of load shedding this summer. The report serves as an indicator of the risk rather than a definitive forecast, due to uncertainties in grids and government interventions.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Summer 2017-2018. Its root_cause is: Projected reliability issues due to changes in generation mix and demand growth pressures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-09-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria, with potential implications later for New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the mentioned regions. Its cause type is: Projected energy reliability issues due to changes in the energy market. Its causes are: Changes in generation mix; increased demand; upcoming retirement of power stations; uncertainties in grid stability and external interventions.

title: What does “Unserved Energy” actually mean – a brief explainer
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "What does “Unserved Energy” actually mean – a brief explainer" posted at 07-09-2017 01:17:00 PM is: The article explains the concept of "Unserved Energy" (USE) in the context of the National Electricity Market (NEM), emphasizing its role in assessing the reliability of electricity supply. USE refers to the energy demand that cannot be met due to insufficient generation or interconnector capacity, leading to load shedding. The AEMO forecasts USE as an expected value across various scenarios, often expressed as a percentage of annual consumption. The NEM reliability standard sets a benchmark that USE should not exceed 0.002% to ensure stability. Assessing USE involves modeling multiple supply-demand scenarios considering generation capacity, interconnector availability, weather, and other factors.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Variability in supply-demand balance and interconnector capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 07-09-2017 01:17:00 PM. Its affected region is: NEM regions within Australia. Its affected key users are: End-customers experiencing load shedding. Its cause type is: System operational considerations. Its causes are: Deficiency in generation capacity, interconnector limitations, variability in demand and supply due to weather and other factors.

title: It’s very misleading to claim “Since the introduction of the National Electricity Market in 1996 the wholesale price of energy has risen dramatically”
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "It’s very misleading to claim “Since the introduction of the National Electricity Market in 1996 the wholesale price of energy has risen dramatically”" posted at 04-09-2017 12:50:00 PM is: The article criticizes a claim made by Allan Hicks of the ETU, which suggests that the introduction of the National Electricity Market (NEM) in 1996 caused a dramatic rise in wholesale energy prices. The author refutes this statement by showing that wholesale prices remained flat for 16 years after the actual start of the NEM in 1998 and only began to escalate around 2015 due to other factors such as droughts and the carbon tax period. The claim linking the price increase to the NEM is deemed inaccurate and misleading, calling into question the understanding of the energy sector by the Electrical Trades Union. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 2015 onwards. Its root_cause is: Misinterpretation of market trends and incorrect historical understanding. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-09-2017. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Large energy users. Its cause type is: Misrepresentation. Its causes are: Inaccurate claims about the relationship between the NEM's introduction and energy price increases, real contributing factors include droughts, carbon tax, and supply/demand conditions.

title: On Saturday 2nd September, AEMO reacted to Severe Weather Warning by imposing limits on interconnector
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "On Saturday 2nd September, AEMO reacted to Severe Weather Warning by imposing limits on interconnector" posted at 03-09-2017 02:20:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) responded to a Severe Weather Warning issued by the Bureau of Meteorology by implementing the constraint set "I-VS_250" on the interconnector to manage power flows, anticipating potential grid issues. This action was influenced by lessons learned from the South Australian blackout in September 2016, showcasing a trend towards more proactive measures against severe weather events. The constraint prevented westward flows, allowing surplus wind-generated power to continue flowing eastward due to high wind output in South Australia, without binding due to non-westward flow conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Severe weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term, as the constraint was applied until weather conditions eased. Its root cause is: Severe weather warning indicating potential grid instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown.
