title: Wind production in South Australia roared out of the June doldrums to a new production record in July 2017
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind production in South Australia roared out of the June doldrums to a new production record in July 2017" posted at 31-08-2017 04:09:00 PM is: The article highlights a significant increase in wind energy production in South Australia during July 2017, marking a new record of nearly 600,000 MWh. This surge in wind energy followed a period of low output in late May and June 2017 due to specific weather patterns. The data is derived from the NEM-Review v6 software tool, illustrating the variability commonly observed in wind energy generation. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: July 2017. Its root cause is: Weather patterns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-08-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Weather pattern changes affecting wind energy production.

title: August Update: Regulatory Responses to Pricing Concern
author: Connor James
classified_content: The summary of the news "August Update: Regulatory Responses to Pricing Concern" posted at 28-08-2017 11:44:00 AM is: The article discusses the increasing energy costs in Australia and the political and regulatory responses to address this issue. Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull has initiated meetings with electricity industry leaders to discuss how retailers can help families obtain better energy deals. The ACCC is conducting a review of the industry with a report expected by June 2018. Various regulatory options, including re-regulation and consumer switching incentives, are being considered to alleviate the pricing pressure on households and businesses. While measures such as standardised information, government comparator websites, and consumer notification practices are aimed at encouraging customers to switch providers, the effectiveness of these efforts remains uncertain. The pressure from rising energy prices highlights the need for a balanced and effective regulatory approach. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (up to mid-2018). Its root_cause is: Regulatory and competitive issues in the electricity retail market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-08-2017. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory and market structure issues. Its causes are: Increased energy costs, lack of consumer switching, dominance of large retailers, insufficient competitive pressure.

title: Cloud patterns on Friday 19th May 2017 – leading to a day of low Solar PV output, NEM-wide
author: James Luffman
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cloud patterns on Friday 19th May 2017 – leading to a day of low Solar PV output, NEM-wide" posted at 25-08-2017 11:34:00 AM is: The article discusses the low solar PV output across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on 19th May 2017 due to widespread and thick cloud cover over eastern Australia. The cloud coverage was significant enough to affect most of the populated areas within the NEM, resulting in unusually low solar power generation, which closely resembled an eclipse-like event. The event's characterization was supported by Solcast’s solar power output data and forecasts, which confirmed the significant dip in power generation for the day. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The day of 19th May 2017 with potential prolonged effects depending on solar PV's persistence as a generation source. Its root_cause is: Widespread thick cloud coverage caused by a slow-moving low-pressure system. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19th May 2017. Its affected region is: Eastern Australia, encompassing significant sections of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Solar PV electricity suppliers and consumers within the NEM. Its cause type is: Meteorological. Its causes are: Slow-moving low-pressure system drawing moisture from northern Australia causing widespread and thick cloud cover over NEM regions on 19th May 2017.

title: A solar eclipse is just one type of a larger class of challenge with solar PV
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A solar eclipse is just one type of a larger class of challenge with solar PV" posted at 23-08-2017 04:18:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of the North American solar eclipse on the Californian grid, highlighting challenges associated with solar PV intermittency, particularly regarding ramp rates, scheduling, and planning for future solar energy integration. While the eclipse was managed well, it flags concerns about future energy scenarios with higher renewable targets. The discussion emphasizes the importance of prediction systems, access to real data, and market structures to incentivize fast response capacity. Additional challenges include data opacity in distributed energy resources and planning for longer-term capacity needs; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term as it pertains to the eclipse event itself, with longer-term implications for planning and integration of solar PV. Its root_cause is: Solar eclipse leading to increased ramp-rate requirements and intermittency challenges for solar PV. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21st August 2017 (eclipse date) and referenced May-June 2017 (decline in solar output). Its affected region is: California and broader North America. Its affected key users are: Electricity grid operators and indirectly, solar energy producers. Its cause type is: Natural event-induced intermittency. Its causes are: Increased ramp-rate requirements, intermittency of solar energy, need for better prediction systems and real data access, and challenges in distributed energy resources management.

title: Energy Users as the “victim” in the train wreck that’s unfolding before our eyes
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Energy Users as the ‘victim’ in the train wreck that’s unfolding before our eyes" posted at 18-08-2017 12:33:00 PM is: The article provides a critical analysis of the escalating electricity prices in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), primarily affecting both residential and business consumers. It highlights government scrutiny of electricity retailers and the media's focus on the high costs impacting consumers, particularly residential users. The discussion extends to concerns about the significant impact on businesses, with high prices threatening their financial viability. The author notes that energy users aren't completely passive victims as some have not taken steps within their control to optimize energy costs. Despite calls for government's involvement, the article suggests a diversified approach involving price comparison, improved energy literacy, and innovations in self-supply solutions can benefit both groups of users.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Rising electricity prices and government scrutiny. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-08-2017. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and business energy users. Its cause type is: Market Dynamics. Its causes are: Rising wholesale and retail electricity prices, inadequate consumer action for cost optimization, and governmental pressure on electricity retailers.

title: [In the NEM, at least] Business consumption of electricity dwarfs residential consumption of electricity
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "In the NEM, at least, Business consumption of electricity dwarfs residential consumption of electricity" posted at 18-08-2017 11:16:00 AM is: The article discusses the disparity between business and residential electricity consumption within the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. According to data from AEMO's 2017 report, business sector electricity consumption was nearly 130,000 GWh in 2016, compared to just under 60,000 GWh for the residential sector in 2017. The speaker attributes this phenomenon to Australia's abundance of natural resources, favorable government policies, and historically low energy costs from coal-fired generation. The article highlights the challenges posed by the energy transition given this high business consumption ratio, which may not be as pronounced in other regions. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The historical development of energy consumption trends and policies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-08-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Business and residential electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Socioeconomic and policy factors. Its causes are: Abundance of natural resources, historically favorable government policies, and reliance on coal-fired generation.

title: Wind Farm output in SA constrained, again, by AEMO’s “System Strength” constraint
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wind Farm output in SA constrained, again, by AEMO’s “System Strength” constraint" posted at 05-08-2017 06:23:00 PM is: During a dispatch interval at 15:45 in the NEM, South Australian wind farms were constrained due to the AEMO's "System Strength" constraint amidst high wind conditions from winter storms. This constraint is a measure implemented post the state-wide blackout on 28th September 2016. The affected timeframe and conditions highlight ongoing challenges with grid stability during extreme weather events. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term, influenced by weather conditions. Its root cause is: High wind conditions and imposed "System Strength" constraint by AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-08-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural events leading to imposed regulatory constraints. Its causes are: High wind conditions, "System Strength" constraint post-September 2016 statewide blackout.

title: Energy prices below zero in South Australia, but Raise Regulation FCAS prices through the roof
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Energy prices below zero in South Australia, but Raise Regulation FCAS prices through the roof" posted at 31-07-2017 05:48:00 PM is: The news reports on a significant event in the electricity market in South Australia where the dispatch price for the energy commodity reached as low as negative $1000/MWh for a five-minute period. At the same time, the "Raise Regulation" Frequency Control Ancillary Service (FCAS) prices increased substantially. This situation highlights a complex interaction within the energy market during the Winter 2017 period in the National Electricity Market (NEM); Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, short-term. Its root_cause is: Imbalance in supply and demand leading to negative pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 31-07-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Energy market participants. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes is: High energy production coupled with low demand leading to negative prices, while FCAS prices soared due to regulation needs.

title: ACCC chief, Rod Sims, includes 6 factors driving retail electricity prices higher
author: Paul McArdle
topic: ACCC inquiry into the National Electricity Market
classified_content: The summary of the news "ACCC chief, Rod Sims, includes 6 factors driving retail electricity prices higher," posted at 28-07-2017 11:56:00 AM is: The article discusses Rod Sims from ACCC outlining six factors contributing to the rise in retail electricity prices. These include reduced competition due to market consolidation, increased network business returns ('gold plating'), excessive reliability standards stemming from overreaction to outages, high gas prices, overly generous green schemes, and an unidentified sixth factor. The discussion came amidst ongoing ACCC inquiries into energy prices, focusing on both retail and wholesale electricity prices, with public forums and a broader inquiry into gas market transparency also taking place. The article reflects a complex scenario beyond simplistic explanations or quick fixes.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium-term, as regulatory and market adjustments may occur over time. Its root_cause is: A combination of reduced market competition, network inefficiencies, high gas prices, and policy impacts. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-07-2017 for article reference; public forum on 25-07-2017; ACCC/AER Conference on 27-28 July 2017; preliminary report due by 27-09-2017; final report by 30-06-2018. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) with a focus on Queensland for certain factors. Its affected key users are: General consumers and businesses participating in the NEM. Its cause type is: Regulatory and market dynamics. Its causes are: Reduced competition, network business returns, overreaction to reliability standards, high gas prices, generous green schemes, and an unidentified factor.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast, of sorts, for the National Electricity Market – Train Wreck upon us?" posted at 20-07-2017 09:48:00 AM is: The article discusses insights shared at the Australian Clean Energy Summit, where the speaker expressed concerns over systemic changes in electricity pricing patterns in the National Electricity Market (NEM). These changes include an increase in high-price events, which challenges strategies like battery storage and demand response, impacting large industrial energy users. The speaker emphasized the complexity of the energy transition and the ongoing challenges it poses.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown (The article provides a current analysis but does not specify an exact timeframe for the impact.). Its root_cause is: Systemic changes in electricity pricing patterns and inadequate processes during the energy transition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 20-07-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market regions including TAS, VIC, and SA. Its affected key users are: Large industrial energy users. Its cause type is: Systemic issues. Its causes is: Increased high-price events in the market impacting energy strategies and large users.

title: Cost Confusion
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cost Confusion" posted at 13-07-2017 12:54:00 PM is: The article discusses the complexities of understanding electricity generation costs and critiques the common metric of the Long-run Cost Of Electricity (LCOE). LCOE is highlighted as not fully capturing the real-world complexities of electricity systems, such as the instantaneous nature of electricity production and consumption, and the fluctuating capacity utilization of different generators. It explains that LCOE often oversimplifies and can be misleading when used to compare different technologies, ignoring factors like dispatchability and system-wide costs. The article argues that while LCOE can be useful for tracking cost trends over time for specific technologies, it is less effective for evaluating their relative economic value within an entire electricity system. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Misunderstandings and limitations in cost evaluation methodologies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-07-2017. Its affected region is: General/Global context, with implications for different electricity markets such as Australia’s NEM. Its affected key users are: Policymakers, energy analysts, and electricity market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Conceptual/Analytical. Its causes are: Misuse of LCOE in evaluating generation technology costs, overlooking dispatchability, system-wide impacts, and real-time production-consumption dynamics.

title: Here’s three big reasons why spot prices in Queensland are higher than they used to be
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Here’s three big reasons why spot prices in Queensland are higher than they used to be" posted at 11-07-2017 03:23:00 PM is: The article discusses the reasons behind the high electricity spot prices in Queensland, attributing them to three main factors: the consolidation of government-owned generation companies, increased regional demand due to coal-seam gas compression for LNG export, and a rise in gas prices affecting power generation. The consolidation led to fewer competitors, allowing greater pricing flexibility, while the increased demand and gas prices have exacerbated the issue. The article emphasizes the need for deeper understanding and communication about these complex electricity market challenges. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term since changes occurred over the years. Its root_cause is: Market structure changes and increased demand for electricity combined with higher gas prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-07-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Market and demand dynamics. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Consolidation of generation companies, increased electricity demand due to coal-seam gas activities, and higher gas prices for generation.

title: The Southern Annular Mode (SAM) contributes to recent wind drought
author: Josh Fisher
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Southern Annular Mode (SAM) contributes to recent wind drought" posted at 07-07-2017 12:31:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of the Southern Annular Mode (SAM) on the observed wind drought in southern Australia during June 2017. Due to a positive phase of SAM, westerly wind belts contracted closer to Antarctica, reducing frontal activity over mainland Australia, which resulted in weaker winds and thus lower wind farm output. The SAM remained in a positive phase as of early July, with potential changes anticipated for later in the month.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant); Its category is: Market reactions; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June to mid-July 2017; Its root_cause is: Positive phase of the Southern Annular Mode (SAM); Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 30th June 2017, beginning of July 2017; Its affected region is: Southern Australia; Its affected key users are: Wind farm operators; Its cause type is: Meteorological/climatic; Its causes are: Positive phase of SAM leading to high atmospheric pressure and reduced wind across southern Australia.

title: If spot price outcomes through Q2 2016 were “truly remarkable” then price outcomes for Q2 2017 were off the chart
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "If spot price outcomes through Q2 2016 were “truly remarkable” then price outcomes for Q2 2017 were off the chart" posted at 06-07-2017 08:47:00 AM is: The news article provides a comprehensive review of electricity spot price outcomes for the second quarter of 2017, highlighting significant increases across various regions in the National Electricity Market (NEM), including Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania, and South Australia. Prices in these regions have escalated to historic highs, surpassing the elevated levels seen in the same period of the previous year and during the drought-affected 2007. The article attributes this rise to a combination of systemic and multifaceted issues, not limited to single events like the closure of power plants such as Hazelwood. It indicates broader implications for the electricity supply chain, affecting generators, retailers, traders, and consumers. The analysis cautions against oversimplified conclusions and emphasizes the importance of understanding the complex dynamics at play within the electricity market.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing from Q2 2017. Its root cause is: Systemic and multifaceted issues within the electricity market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-07-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions including Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, Tasmania, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Generators, retailers, traders, and large energy users. Its cause type is: Economic/market factors. Its causes are: Closure of coal-fired power plants, high gas prices, market instability, and supply-demand imbalances.

title: Where’s the wind gone?  NEM-wide wind farm operation lowest in 5 years (maybe ever, on like-for-like basis?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Where’s the wind gone? NEM-wide wind farm operation lowest in 5 years (maybe ever, on like-for-like basis?)" posted at 30-06-2017 03:21:00 PM is: The article discusses an unprecedented decrease in wind farm production across the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia during June 2017, marking the lowest output in over five years. Contributing factors include notably low wind speeds, technical issues such as rotor bearing problems at the Boco Rock Wind Farm, and constraints on wind farm output in South Australia. This situation has led Infigen to lower its revenue and production forecasts. The article suggests the need for more sophisticated planning and policy adjustments to ensure reliable wind energy as the NEM transitions to renewable sources. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Low wind speeds, technical issues, and output constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: June 2017, with emphasis on specific low output periods in May and June. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity operators and consumers reliant on wind energy. Its cause type is: Environmental and technical. Its causes are: Low wind speeds, technical problems at Boco Rock Wind Farm, and output constraints in South Australia.

title: Another industrial energy user enters Liquidation (in South Australia this time)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Another industrial energy user enters Liquidation (in South Australia this time)" posted at 26-06-2017 03:21:00 PM is: The article discusses the liquidation of an industrial energy user in South Australia, highlighting electricity prices as a significant contributing factor. Electricity price volatility, stemming from issues like regional price shocks and security-of-supply concerns, has exacerbated since Q2 2016, posing risks to industrial users. Despite attempts to manage these risks through strategies like demand response, the energy user ultimately went into liquidation. The situation underscores a broader challenge of managing energy transitions and maintaining energy supply and price stability. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium-term, considering immediate price impacts and potential lasting effects on industrial demand. Its root_cause is: High electricity prices and volatility. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-06-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Industrial energy users in the region. Its cause type is: Economic/market dynamics. Its causes are: Price shocks, security-of-supply issues, ineffective demand response implementation, and broader market instability.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Package of “reduce electricity prices in QLD” measures seen to reduce hedge contract prices" posted at 06-06-2017 03:27:00 PM is: The Queensland Government announced a series of measures aimed at reducing electricity prices in the region. Following these announcements, a decrease in Base Load futures prices was observed. The actions include maximizing output from Stanwell Corporation while maintaining profitability and the potential reactivation of the Swanbank E power plant, which may impact both electricity and gas prices.. title: Energy Supply Disruption – Understand the Customer Drivers
author: Jim Snow
classified_content: The summary of the news "Energy Supply Disruption – Understand the Customer Drivers" posted at 02-06-2017 12:13:00 PM is: The energy supply industry is undergoing major disruption caused by simultaneous high prices in electricity and gas, leading to significant restructuring of energy-intensive industries and a decline in energy demand. This shift is driven by customer demand for more sustainable and predictable energy alternatives such as solar PV and energy storage, as well as state-driven policies focused on emission reduction. The disruption is compounded by grid security concerns, potential gas shortages, and inadequate forecasting methodologies. State policies, rather than national directives, are predominantly influencing the energy landscape, reflecting an ideological commitment to sustainability despite short-term impacts on price and reliability. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term impact as the restructuring and transition to alternative energy sources progress. Its root_cause is: High electricity and gas prices and policy shifts towards renewable energy. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-06-2017 12:13:00 PM. Its affected region is: Predominantly state-level in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy-intensive industries, tourism, food processing, and residential consumers. Its cause type is: Economic and policy-driven disruption. Its causes are: High commodity prices in electricity and gas, state-driven emission reduction policies, inadequate forecasting methods, consumer shift to self-generation and renewable energy sources.

The report, prepared by Dr. Alan Finkel and his team, was a response to the state-wide blackout in South Australia on 28th September 2016 and aimed to address issues concerning the security and future of the national electricity market in Australia. title: Warnings of load shedding in Victoria and South Australia on Thursday 11th May, and larger on Monday 15th May
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Warnings of load shedding in Victoria and South Australia on Thursday 11th May, and larger on Monday 15th May" posted at 08-05-2017 09:56:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued warnings about potential load shedding in Victoria and South Australia due to supply constraints and scheduled maintenance at the Loy Yang A power station. The load shedding is forecast for Thursday 11th May, with a larger impact expected on Monday 15th May. Victoria may experience more than 10% of Melbourne's demand being unmet, while South Australia's shortage is primarily predicted for the morning of 15th May. These disruptions highlight challenges in the energy transition, exacerbated by various stakeholder actions and low wind generation output. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 11th May to 15th May, 2017. Its root_cause is: Planned maintenance at Loy Yang A and low wind output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11th May 2017 and 15th May 2017. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Supply constraints and forecast low wind generation. Its causes are: Maintenance at Loy Yang A, low wind output contributed to projected shortages.

title: More trouble brewing at Loy Yang A power station – with strike action called
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More trouble brewing at Loy Yang A power station – with strike action called" posted at 04-05-2017 06:53:00 PM is: The Loy Yang A power station in Victoria, operated by AGL, faces potential shutdown following a decision by 15 members of the Electrical Trades Union (ETU) to strike from May 15th. This industrial action occurs amidst a turbulent business environment. The announcement of the closure decision was made between the data update times of 12:00 and 15:00 MT PASA Region Availability and was communicated by AEMO. This potential shutdown, coupled with recent market events like the Hazelwood closure, has led to elevated futures prices for quarters 2, 3, and 4 as compared to Q1, as observed in market reaction trends. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, covering the days leading up to and following May 15th as market reactions unfold. Its root_cause is: Industrial action by ETU members. Its accident scale is: Potential large-scale impact due to power station closure. Its dates are: 04-05-2017, with the strike commencing on 15-05-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in the NEM. Its cause type is: Industrial relations issue. Its causes are: Decision by 15 ETU members to strike, leading to the announcement and market notification of a potential shutdown of the Loy Yang A power station.

title: More about wind output and AEMO’s intervention in South Australia 25-26 April
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "More about wind output and AEMO’s intervention in South Australia 25-26 April" posted at 01-05-2017 02:55:00 PM is: The article discusses a peak in wind generation output in South Australia, reaching a record 1,541 MW on April 25, causing the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to intervene to maintain system security. With over 80% of local demand being met by renewables for much of the period, the capacity of interconnectors allowed energy export to Victoria. However, AEMO had to address system strength concerns by directing gas turbines at Hallett Power Station to come online as thermal generation was nearing minimum operational levels with Pelican Point going offline and only one Torrens Island B unit operational. The intervention further involved starting additional units at Torrens Island and managing output at other thermal stations to ensure system stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate (April 25-26, 2017). Its root cause is: High wind generation and low thermal generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: April 25-26, 2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Renewable energy generation variability. Its causes are: Record wind generation, low operational thermal units, system strength requirements, AEMO intervention for system stability.

title: Aggregate rate of wind production across South Australia breaks the all-time record
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aggregate rate of wind production across South Australia breaks the all-time record" posted at 26-04-2017 12:38:00 PM is: The article reports on a record-breaking wind production in South Australia, where the aggregate wind output reached 1,540MW at 23:45 NEM time. This increase in wind generation led the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to issue Market Notices directing market participant operations to maintain system security, likely due to the windy conditions impacting the grid stability. The direction was expected to remain until noon on April 26th, 2017. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until 12:00 on 26-04-2017. Its root_cause is: High wind production necessitating grid interventions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-04-2017 to 26-04-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural (weather-related). Its causes are: Record high wind production, grid stability interventions by AEMO.

title: Aggregate Wind Farm output across South Australia topped 1,400MW for the first time – on 9th April, with more to come
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aggregate Wind Farm output across South Australia topped 1,400MW for the first time – on 9th April, with more to come" posted at 24-04-2017 12:30:00 PM is: The wind farm output across South Australia has reached a record of 1,434MW earlier in April 2017, thanks to the addition of the Hornsdale site. Forecasts indicate further high wind production on 26th April, potentially reaching 1,320MW. This increase in wind energy has driven negative spot price outcomes due to high supply. The data reflects the ongoing challenge of managing energy diversity, especially post-Hazelwood closure. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to Medium term. Its root_cause is: Increased wind farm output in South Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 9th April 2017 to 26th April 2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Renewable energy production capacity. Its causes are: Increase in wind power generation due to the Hornsdale wind farm addition and wind conditions.

title: High Regulation FCAS Prices on Tuesday 18th April in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "High Regulation FCAS Prices on Tuesday 18th April in South Australia" posted at 21-04-2017 11:35:00 AM is: On April 18, 2017, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared Administered Pricing for market ancillary services in South Australia due to changes in the transmission network that resulted in new Constraint Sets and a reduction in the transfer capability on the Heywood link. This led to a significant spike in the Raise Regulation FCAS price to $14,000/MWh, where it remained for several hours. The scarcity of generators offering FCAS in the region contributed to the price increase, with Pelican Point setting the price at the Market Price Cap due to the constrained interconnector flow and limited alternatives. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: The impacts started on 18th April 2017 and lasted several hours. The root cause is: Changes in the underlying transmission network leading to new Constraint Sets and reduced transfer capability. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: 18-04-2017. The affected region is: South Australia. The affected key users are: Unknown. The cause type is: Grid frequency issues and market pricing regulations. The causes are: Transmission network changes, new Constraint Sets, reduced Heywood link capability, limited FCAS providers in South Australia, and Pelican Point setting prices high.

title: Ararat Wind Farm at full capacity
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Ararat Wind Farm at full capacity" posted at 19-04-2017 01:52:00 PM is: The Ararat Wind Farm in Victoria, Australia, has reached full capacity, contributing to significant daily wind production in the region. On its best day, April 10th, 2017, the farm produced 4,300 MWh, equivalent to continuous operation of a 180 MW power plant for a day. Though production levels have varied, this development marks a notable milestone in Victoria's wind energy sector, supporting broader targets such as the ACT Government's 100% renewable energy goal. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short term. Its root cause is: Increase in wind farm capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10th April 2017; 31st August 2016. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Capacity expansion. Its causes are: Reaching full capacity at Ararat Wind Farm; contributing to daily wind production in Victoria.

title: Noteworthy analysis of the state-wide blackout across South Australia on 28th September 2016
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2016 - 28th September - State-Wide Blackout in South Australia
classified_content: The summary of the news "Noteworthy analysis of the state-wide blackout across South Australia on 28th September 2016" posted at 30-03-2017 03:54:00 PM is: The article provides a comprehensive overview of the reports, analyses, and commentary related to the statewide blackout in South Australia on 28th September 2016. It consolidates various materials and publications from AEMO, AER, AEMC, and other organizations, detailing the investigation into the causes, consequences, and recommended strategies to prevent similar incidents in the future. The event highlighted the challenges in maintaining system stability and the need for regulatory and operational improvements. Reports from entities like AEMO and AER have detailed the systemic failures and responses, leading to ongoing regulatory reviews and legal actions against certain wind farm operators. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to several months as investigations and systemic changes were implemented over time. Its root_cause is: System-wide instability exacerbated by failures in wind farm settings and insufficient generator-supplied inertia. Its accident scale is: State-wide. Its dates are: 28th September 2016 (incident date), 5th October 2016, 19th October 2016, 12th December 2016, 28th March 2017 (report publication dates), among others. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: General population and industries in South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical and regulatory failures. Its causes are: High wind conditions leading to a series of faults in the electricity network, inadequate settings in wind farm generators, and insufficient inertia in the system.

title: Adding a Slice of Cheese
author: Bruce Miller
topic: 2016 - 28th September - State-Wide Blackout in South Australia
classified_content: The summary of the news "Adding a Slice of Cheese" posted at 30-03-2017 11:23:00 AM is: The article discusses the South Australian Blackout that occurred on 28th September 2016, highlighting the poor system frequency control as a contributing factor. The analysis suggests that better frequency control, specifically focusing on generator governor settings, might have averted the blackout. The failure of rapid response from synchronous generators at the Torrens Island Power station due to the FCAS market structure contributed to the blackout. The writer advocates for a simpler approach to frequency control that does not rely solely on market mechanisms. Instead, there should be rules requiring generators to contribute to frequency control, ensuring system security and stability across the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing implications for policy reform. Its root_cause is: Poor frequency control and inadequate generator response. Its accident scale is: State-wide blackout. Its dates are: 28-09-2016. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Inadequate frequency control, inappropriate generator governor settings, delayed response from FCAS market contributors.

title: End of an era at Hazelwood
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "End of an era at Hazelwood" posted at 30-03-2017 10:32:00 AM is: The closure of Hazelwood Power Station in Victoria, announced in November 2016 and executed sequentially from March 26 to March 29, 2017, marks a significant step in the decline of coal-fired power generation in Australia. This decision, influenced by climate change concerns and economic pressures, challenges the electricity sector with tighter supply-demand balance and anticipated higher electricity prices, particularly evident in the futures market for Q1 2018. While the shutdown highlights a push towards ecological sustainability, it also poses risks to supply security and price stability, demanding careful management of the energy transition and adaptive responses from the market; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Announcement and execution of the closure of Hazelwood Power Station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: From announcement on 3 November 2016 to closure completion on 29 March 2017. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy-intensive industrial facilities, electricity consumers in Victoria. Its cause type is: Planned closure driven by policy and economic factors. Its causes are: Climate change concerns, emissions intensity of Hazelwood, economic unfeasibility, and structural decline of coal-fired generation.

title: [POSTED 13:40] TC Debbie dampens electricity consumption in Queensland
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "TC Debbie dampens electricity consumption in Queensland" posted at 28-03-2017 01:45:00 PM is: Tropical Cyclone Debbie significantly reduced electricity consumption in the North and Ross zones of Queensland, as the cyclone caused lower temperatures that reduced air-conditioning demand, shutdowns of businesses and schools, and potential power cuts affecting 38,000 customers. The cyclone's path between Mackay and Townsville also led to decreased solar PV output due to cloud cover, further reducing apparent consumption figures. The article highlights a minimum reduction of 400MW in demand in these zones, influenced by complex interactions of natural events and human responses. The cyclone demonstrated challenges linked to distributed energy solutions in storm conditions, emphasizing safety measures and infrastructure resilience. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short term impact during and shortly after the cyclone's passage. Its root_cause is: Impact of TC Debbie on regional weather and electricity infrastructure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-03-2017 to 28-03-2017. Its affected region is: North and Ross zones in Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential, commercial, and industrial electricity consumers in the region. Its cause type is: Natural disaster (cyclone). Its causes are: Lower temperatures reducing demand, shutdowns of businesses and schools, power cuts due to downed power lines, and reduced solar PV output due to cloud cover.

title: A sudden series of SA spikes
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "A sudden series of SA spikes" posted at 23-03-2017 03:48:00 PM is: The article discusses a period of spot price volatility in South Australia's electricity market that occurred on a Tuesday between 10:30am and 1pm. The primary cause of this volatility was a significant transmission line outage on the Victorian network, which severely limited the Heywood Interconnector to only 50 MW of imports into South Australia, far below its usual capacity of 650 MW. This, combined with low wind generation and tight import restrictions, led to high price spikes in electricity as peaking plants adjusted their bidding strategies to capitalize on the market conditions. The strategic rebidding by these peaking plants further exacerbated the volatility, causing the price to fluctuate between high and low extremes. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, occurring during the specified timeframe on Tuesday. Its root_cause is: Transmission line outage on the 500kV Victorian network. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-03-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System operational constraints. Its causes are: Transmission line outage, limited interconnector capacity, low wind generation, strategic rebidding by peaking plants.

title: NEM FCAS causer pays factor issues for wind and solar farms
author: Harley MacKenzie
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM FCAS causer pays factor issues for wind and solar farms" posted at 23-03-2017 11:35:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges faced by wind and solar farms in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) concerning the Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) causer pays factors (CPFs). Since 2014, the cost of FCAS regulation has significantly risen, disproportionately affecting wind and solar generators due to inappropriate CPF formulations originally designed for other generator types. These formulations unfairly measure deviations for semi-dispatched units, not required to meet specific dispatch targets, leading to excessive regulation costs. The AEMO forecasting systems, AWEFS and ASEFS, contribute to large forecasting errors, producing outlier CPFs that aggravate costs. The article suggests that implementing an “Estimated Power” forecast system for wind and solar farms could rectify these issues by allowing more accurate generation predictions and reducing financial burdens on these renewable sources.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: fuel prices. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Inadequate CPF formulation and poor forecasting for wind and solar generations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 23-03-2017 11:35:00 AM . Its affected region is: South Australia and potentially other regions within NEM. Its affected key users are: Wind and solar farm operators within the NEM. Its cause type is: Systematic and procedural inadequacies. Its causes are: Increased FCAS regulation costs, inappropriate CPF calculations, use of poor forecasts (AWEFS and ASEFS), high financial liabilities for wind and solar farms.

title: Fast Frequency Service – Treating the symptom not the cause?
author: Kate Summers
classified_content: The summary of the news "Fast Frequency Service – Treating the symptom not the cause?" posted at 23-03-2017 10:58:00 AM is: The article discusses the challenges in maintaining stable frequency control in the National Electricity Market (NEM) due to changes in regulatory frameworks for frequency control that have compromised system resilience. These changes, such as the shift to market-based control and increased governor deadbands, result in inefficient and delayed frequency responses. The paper calls for reassessment of the regulatory approach, advocating for narrow mandated deadbands and removing disincentives for tight governor control to improve system stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing since regulatory changes in 1999 and 2001 with specific events noted in 2017. Its root_cause is: Regulatory changes and disincentives for tight governor control. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-03-2017 (main date), 10-02-2017 (when hot weather caused issues in NSW). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), specifically noted in NSW on 10th February 2017. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and providers within the NEM. Its cause type is: Regulatory and technical changes. Its causes are: Changes in frequency control, increase in governor deadbands, market-based control mechanisms, and regulatory disincentives for effective primary control.

title: Let’s talk about FCAS
author: Jonathon Dyson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Let’s talk about FCAS" posted at 23-03-2017 10:29:00 AM is: The article delves into Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) and their integral role in maintaining grid stability by ensuring the power system operates at a frequency of 50Hz. It highlights the transformation of FCAS from a seldom-considered aspect to a pivotal topic in energy discussions, especially following significant pricing changes. The article focuses on how demand and generation adjustments maintain frequency balance and how FCAS regulation and contingency services address these fluctuations. It dispels myths about the inability of renewable sources like wind and solar to provide ancillary services, explaining that economic factors often dictate participation. Recent market trends in South Australia are referenced to illustrate the market signals for FCAS. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant); Its category is: Grid frequency issues; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing; Its root_cause is: Increased focus and discussion on grid stability and frequency management in energy markets; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 23-03-2017; Its affected region is: Australia, with references to international contexts; Its affected key users are: Energy utilities, major consumers, politicians, and media outlets; Its cause type is: Market and regulation changes; Its causes are: Increased demand for grid stability solutions, market pricing changes, integration of renewable sources into FCAS.

title: On batteries, and “fixing South Australia’s problems”
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "On batteries, and 'fixing South Australia's problems'" posted at 16-03-2017 06:49:00 AM is: The article discusses Tesla's proposition, led by Elon Musk, to address South Australia's electricity challenges with a 100MW battery storage solution. Several existing issues in South Australia are examined, including high-high prices, system blackout events, and load shedding instances, with an emphasis on the load shedding event on February 8, 2017, for which the 100MW battery solution seems most applicable. The piece debates whether Tesla's solution could effectively fix these problems or whether the marketing around the proposal has overly optimistic expectations. Furthermore, the article delves into technical aspects and the perceived confusion between power measures—MW and MWh. Finally, it invites interested parties to engage with an ongoing Energy Storage Register project to enhance the electricity storage sector in Australia. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term, particularly in addressing load shedding events. Its root_cause is: Issues with electricity supply and demand balance, and specific load shedding events in South Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-03-2017; references to past dates include the blackout on 28th September 2016 and load shedding on 8th February 2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Large energy consumers and businesses in South Australia affected by high electricity prices and supply disruptions. Its cause type is: Supply-demand imbalance and electricity market challenges. Its causes are: High-high prices, statewide blackout on 28th September 2016, load shedding on 8th February 2017, and other local electricity stability issues in South Australia.

title: With the closure of Hazelwood looming, hedge contract prices climb skywards
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "With the closure of Hazelwood looming, hedge contract prices climb skywards" posted at 14-03-2017 11:23:00 AM is: The approaching closure of the Hazelwood power station has led to a significant increase in Victorian hedge contract price levels, reflecting heightened market volatility. Over the preceding months, factors such as a state-wide blackout in South Australia and recent load shedding events have contributed to the rise, signaling a consensus about challenging market dynamics for Q2 2017. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term (focused around the upcoming Q2 2017). Its root_cause is: The imminent closure of the Hazelwood power station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-03-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and plant closure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Closure of Hazelwood power station, volatility in South Australian power prices, load shedding in South Australia.

title: Where is the east coast domgas development boom?
author: Graeme Bethune
classified_content: The summary of the news "Where is the east coast domgas development boom?" posted at 07-03-2017 02:56:00 PM is: The article discusses the lack of a domestic gas development boom in Australia's east coast, despite a significant increase in gas prices. Factors contributing to this shortage include the slump in oil prices since 2014, high development costs, moratoriums and bans on exploration, political and sovereign risks, market concentration and transparency issues, and undeveloped reserves held by a few companies. These challenges prevent new projects from emerging, even as existing reserves deplete, leaving the supply-demand balance constrained. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Fuel prices. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: A combination of price instabilities, regulatory challenges, and undeveloped gas fields. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: 07-03-2017 02:56:00 PM. The affected region is: Australia's east coast. The affected key users are: Gas companies and domestic gas consumers. The cause type is: Economic and regulatory. The causes are: Slump in oil prices, high development costs, restrictions on exploration, political risks, market concentration, lack of transparency, and undeveloped reserves.

title: Was South Australia lucky the lights stayed on last Friday?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Was South Australia lucky the lights stayed on last Friday?" posted at 06-03-2017 02:39:00 PM is: The article discusses an incident on 3 March 2017, when a failure at the Torrens Island B switchyard led to the tripping of three units at the Torrens Island Power Station, resulting in a loss of over 400 MW of power, approximately 17% of South Australia's electricity demand at the time. This caused a near-simultaneous trip at the Pelican Point Power Station, further reducing output by 220 MW. Despite these losses, the electricity system remained stable without widespread load shedding, possibly due to a mysterious simultaneous drop in demand and increased interconnector flow, particularly on Heywood. This incident has similarities to the SA Black System event on 28 September the previous year, which led to a complete system shutdown. Investigations into the root cause and implications for system security are ongoing.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate with potential long-term implications. The root_cause is: Equipment failure and subsequent power station trips. Its accident scale is: Significant, impacting a large portion of the state's electricity output. Its dates are: 03-03-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers across South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical failure and systemic response. Its causes are: Equipment failure at Torrens Island, tripping of Pelican Point, unexpected demand drop, high interconnector flow.

title: [UPDATED] AEMO Market Notice on “High Impact Outage” for tomorrow – and possible effect in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "[UPDATED] AEMO Market Notice on “High Impact Outage” for tomorrow – and possible effect in South Australia" posted at 06-03-2017 01:54:00 PM is: The article discusses an AEMO Market Notice predicting potential electricity disruptions in South Australia on March 7th due to a "High Impact Outage." There is a risk of Automatic Underfrequency Load Shedding (AUFLS) if the other Heywood line trips, potentially islanding South Australia and necessitating load shedding to maintain system stability. This risk coincides with ongoing concerns due to recent transformer explosions and unit trips at Torrens and Pelican Point. The AEMO forecast a spike in electricity prices on March 7th, linked to higher demand and reduced wind farm production during a critical period. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: March 7, 2017. Its root cause is: Possible tripping of the Heywood line leading to system instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: March 6, 2017 (post date) and March 7, 2017 (event date). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System instability due to infrastructure concerns. Its causes are: Possible tripping of another Heywood line, transformer explosion, and unit trips at Torrens and Pelican Point, potential use of AUFLS to stabilize the grid.

title: Collective skittishness about South Australia?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Collective skittishness about South Australia?" posted at 01-03-2017 10:04:00 AM is: This article discusses ongoing concerns regarding electricity supply and pricing in South Australia, particularly following a series of significant events such as high prices in 2016 and multiple blackouts in late 2016 and early 2017. It highlights that the current anxiety in the region is partly fueled by politicization surrounding the energy transition and a general decline in emotional resilience concerning electricity supplies. The analysis also notes discrepancies in demand forecasts and their impact on market behavior, pointing out a dependence on energy imports from Victoria. These factors, combined with the upcoming closure of Hazelwood power station in Victoria, exacerbate the challenges facing the region. The article calls for reasoned approaches to understanding and addressing the root causes rather than political quick fixes. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability and fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing implications on supply and pricing. Its root cause is: System instability coupled with politicization of energy policies. Its accident scale is: Regional significance given the widespread concerns and incidents. Its dates are: 01-03-2017, along with mentioned dates of 28th September, 1st December, and 8th February. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: General consumers and businesses in South Australia reliant on stable electricity supply. Its cause type is: Systemic and political issues leading to instability. Its causes are: High electricity prices, recent blackouts, reduced surplus generation forecast, dependency on Victoria for supply, politicization of the energy transition.

title: Autumn 2017 in the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Autumn 2017 in the NEM" posted at 01-03-2017 08:47:00 AM is: The article serves as a collection of various pieces written about the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the autumn of 2017; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-03-2017. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes are: Unknown.

title: A personal experience of spot-price paid solar injections
author: Dan Lee
topic: Personal experiences with Distributed Energy Resources
classified_content: The summary of the news "A personal experience of spot-price paid solar injections" posted at 28-02-2017 03:50:00 PM is: The article provides a personal account from Jamey, an employee affected by the demise of the start-up retailer Urth Energy, which offered spot pricing for residential solar customers. Despite missing out on favorable government tariffs, Jamey optimized his energy return by engaging with different energy retailers. Following Urth Energy's collapse, communication and transition issues highlighted flaws in the "Retailer of Last Resort" arrangements, suggesting the need for industry improvement amid market volatility and new retailer entrants. The potential causes for Urth Energy's failure are speculated to be cash flow shortages or spot market volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Residential satisfaction. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term instability during and immediately after Urth Energy's market exit. Its root cause is: Market operational challenges and financial instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st February 2017 to 16th February 2017. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential solar PV customers. Its cause type is: Market and operational failure. Its causes are: Cash flow issues and exposure to spot market volatility.

title: EnerNOC seems to have grossly under-represented the amount of Demand Response active in the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "EnerNOC seems to have grossly under-represented the amount of Demand Response active in the NEM" posted at 23-02-2017 10:52:00 AM is: The article discusses concerns over the alleged underestimation of demand response capacity in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It critiques a reported figure of only 235 MW of active demand response, suggesting it is a significant underrepresentation compared to other markets and to what EnerNOC itself is involved in supplying. The article implies that a better understanding and acknowledgment of the demand response potential in the NEM could contribute positively to managing electricity price volatility and reliability, especially during high-demand periods like the summer of 2016-17. The piece also reflects on the dynamics between established market players and innovative start-ups in proposing new solutions like the "negawatt buyback model" for demand response. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term during high-demand periods, as seen in the summer of 2016-17. Its root cause is: Underestimation of demand response potential and market dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-02-2017 10:52:00 AM. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and demand response providers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Reporting and market analysis. Its causes are: Underreported demand response capacity, potential market dynamics between incumbents and start-ups, and the proposal of new demand response models.

title: Question – in an interconnected electricity system (and market), when did it become acceptable to neglect a systems view?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Question – in an interconnected electricity system (and market), when did it become acceptable to neglect a systems view?" posted at 23-02-2017 10:02:00 AM is: The article discusses the complexity and interconnectedness of the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), emphasizing how changes within the system can have unforeseen consequences. It highlights the importance of considering a systems view to avoid unintended outcomes and underscores the impact of short-term market volatility on contract prices. The interconnected nature of the NEM is often overlooked, which can lead to significant consequences over time, necessitating further analysis and discussion.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unintended consequences of interconnected market changes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-02-2017 10:02:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Unknown.
