title: Small solar owners saved the NSW electricity market $888 million during heatwave
author: Marija Petkovic
classified_content: The summary of the news "Small solar owners saved the NSW electricity market $888 million during heatwave" posted at 22-02-2017 03:58:00 PM is: During a severe heatwave from February 9-11, 2017, in New South Wales, small solar photovoltaic systems generated approximately 17 GWh of power, accounting for 2% of the state's electricity needs. This significantly helped in reducing electricity prices and preventing possible involuntary load shedding. It was estimated that without this small solar contribution, the electricity market costs would have been $888 million higher, as small solar helped reduce peak demand and dramatically lowered wholesale electricity prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and significant during the heatwave. Its root cause is: Extreme heatwave conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 9-11 February 2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Extreme temperatures exceeding 45°C leading to increased electricity demand and stress on the electricity market.

title: How tight demand/supply situations can make solar forecasting important
author: Nick Engerer
classified_content: The summary of the news "How tight demand/supply situations can make solar forecasting important" posted at 21-02-2017 12:21:00 PM is: During an intense heatwave in February 2017, New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland experienced significant electricity demand spikes, with solar energy from rooftop systems providing essential load reduction. However, the inadequacy in solar power forecasting due to fast-changing cloud cover was highlighted, with the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) predicting a 310MW generation shortfall, barely mitigated by solar contributions. This unpredictability spurred the founding of Solcast, a solar forecasting company aiming to improve future power output predictions through advanced satellite data and machine learning. This innovation is expected to stabilize the power system as reliance on solar energy grows. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term during the heatwave, particularly the afternoon and early evening of 10th February 2017. Its root_cause is: Inadequate solar power forecasting due to fast-changing weather conditions and cloud cover. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electric utilities, grid operators, and consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Heatwave driving high electricity demand, unpredictable cloud cover impacting solar power output, lack of real-time accurate solar forecasting models leading to potential supply shortfalls.

title: Five fixes for the NEM
author: Greg Houston
classified_content: The summary of the news "Five fixes for the NEM" posted at 20-02-2017 03:07:00 PM is: The article discusses the recent challenges facing the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, particularly during the summer of 2016-17, where extreme weather conditions led to load shedding in South Australia and near shortages in New South Wales and Queensland. The market's current "energy only" design, compounded by the Renewable Energy Target (RET) and high domestic gas prices, has resulted in a sub-optimal generation mix with insufficient reliable capacity. With the upcoming closure of the Hazelwood plant, the article suggests five measures to improve market stability and reliability, such as reviewing AEMO protocols, increasing interconnection between regions, eliminating RET's structural disadvantages on reliable generation, adjusting market price caps, and easing gas exploration moratoria. These changes aim to balance the goals of decarbonisation with the need for a reliable electricity supply. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing until addressed. Its root_cause is: Sub-optimal generation portfolio and market design issues exacerbated by policy interventions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-02-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia, New South Wales, Queensland. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the NEM regions. Its cause type is: Market design and policy challenges. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Load shedding, near shortages during extreme weather, high gas prices, RET-induced market distortions, closure of coal plants.

title: Interesting take, by battery maker Sonnen, on the “free energy” business model
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Interesting take, by battery maker Sonnen, on the 'free energy' business model" posted at 20-02-2017 08:40:00 AM is: The article discusses battery manufacturer Sonnen’s innovative business model that offers customers free energy in exchange for the right to export excess energy from their residential systems during peak periods of grid or market stress, highlighting the impact of such models on the electricity sector amid ongoing energy transitions. The focus is on the implications of these innovations, particularly in the National Electricity Market (NEM), and the potential benefits of demand response and battery storage technologies, which are gaining traction. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. The root_cause is: Introduction of a new business model in the energy market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-02-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Residential customers with battery storage systems. Its cause type is: Business model innovation. Its causes are: Introduction of free energy provision by Sonnen, emphasizing battery storage and demand response technology in response to energy market transitions.

title: Key Events Timeline – NSW Friday 10 Feb 2017
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Key Events Timeline – NSW Friday 10 Feb 2017" posted at 16-02-2017 11:46:00 AM is: The article details critical events and announcements impacting the electricity market in New South Wales, Australia, on Friday, February 10, 2017. It outlines a series of Lack of Reserve (LOR) notices issued by AEMO, indicating periods when electricity supply did not meet demand forecasts. These LOR conditions varied throughout the day, with instances of LOR3 (actual or forecast negative reserves), which triggered curtailment and other market interventions, such as directing the Tomago aluminium smelter to reduce consumption temporarily. Updates on demand forecasts, power station output, and dispatch price spikes are provided, noting the Tallawarra power station plant failure and the actions at Colongra gas turbines. Despite managing to avert large-scale load shedding, the market experienced pressures due to high demand and supply constraints.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and short-term, as the events occurred within a single day and the grid instability issues were resolved by the evening. Its root cause is: High electricity demand and insufficient reserve generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Significant, affecting a large power-consuming facility like the Tomago aluminium smelter and requiring intervention measures. Its dates are: 10-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Industrial users, specifically the Tomago aluminium smelter. Its cause type is: Operational and system constraints. Its causes are: High demand, plant failure at Tallawarra, constraints in generation and interconnector capacities, and the curtailment at Tomago smelter.

title: Examining why Pelican Point didn’t bid full capacity into the NEM on Wednesday 8 February
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Examining why Pelican Point didn’t bid full capacity into the NEM on Wednesday 8 February" posted at 13-02-2017 07:59:00 PM is: The article discusses the circumstances surrounding Pelican Point Power Station's decision not to bid full capacity into the National Electricity Market (NEM) during a period of load shedding in South Australia on February 8, 2017. The gas-fired power station, owned by Engie, did not bid its full capacity due to insufficient gas supply contracts that would have allowed it to operate both its gas turbines simultaneously. Commercially, Engie had no incentive to enter long-term gas supply arrangements beyond one turbine as market conditions did not justify such decisions economically. Despite having physical capacity, the second unit was not utilized due to lack of guaranteed gas supply at necessary rates. On February 9, under direction from the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), the second turbine operated, which ensured cost coverage. The article highlights the challenges of aligning commercial decisions with electricity grid demands, and the role AEMO plays in system security. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Insufficient gas supply contracts and market conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-02-2017 and 09-02-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Market-driven decision. Its causes are: Insufficient gas contracts, high cost of ad-hoc gas supply, uncertainties in electricity prices.

title: [4th Post] Large and rapid demand drop curtails demand bid for new record in Queensland
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Large and rapid demand drop curtails demand bid for new record in Queensland" posted at 13-02-2017 06:27:00 PM is: A significant drop in scheduled demand by 600MW occurred in Queensland with two sequential reductions, attributed most likely to storm fronts affecting the region. Storms provided relief from heat and potential network outages, while other possible causes included demand response from large industrial users or small-scale providers and an AEMO-recognized "Non-credible contingency event" affecting transmission lines; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Storm fronts and possible transmission line trip. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-02-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Industrial and small-scale energy users. Its cause type is: Natural and technical. Its causes are: Storm-induced demand reduction, potential network outages, possible transmission line trip, and mild demand response.

title: [3rd post] The supply/demand situation in Queensland gets tighter
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The supply/demand situation in Queensland gets tighter" posted at 13-02-2017 01:40:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenging electricity supply-demand situation in Queensland during the Summer 2016-17, highlighting record-high demand levels and the impacts of a short-notice transmission outage. At 13:20, the dispatch target demand reached 9,155MW and was forecasted to hit a record of 9,538MW by 17:00. Meanwhile, the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin was critically low at just above 10%, with less than 1,000MW of spare capacity. A transmission outage in Powerlink’s network, affecting the high-voltage line from Tarong to Mt England, added to the tensions by reducing redundancy. Additionally, solar output was lower than the previous day due to cloud cover, with a peak production of 1,000MW expected to decline rapidly towards the evening.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate (same day). Its root cause is: High electricity demand and transmission outage. Its accident scale is: Regional (affecting Queensland). Its dates are: 13-02-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Power consumers in Queensland, particularly in Brisbane. Its cause type is: Operational and demand-related. Its causes are: Record high electricity demand, low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin, transmission outage on the Tarong to Mt England line, and reduced solar generation due to cloud cover.

title: [Post 2] Later AEMO forecasts moderate demand forecasts slightly – but still looks to be massive
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Later AEMO forecasts moderate demand forecasts slightly – but still looks to be massive" posted at 13-02-2017 12:43:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) forecasts for electricity demand during a summer day in the National Electricity Market (NEM) showed some fluctuations with demand for the afternoon predicted to reach 9,480 MW, slightly above the previous record. The actual demand earlier in the day exceeded forecasts, while a tight supply/demand balance contributed to elevated prices, indicated by an Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin below 15%. AEMO re-issued the LOR1 notice as a market response strategy amidst ongoing electricity supply challenges in states like South Australia, New South Wales, and Queensland. The forecast adjustments highlight the inherent challenges in demand prediction and the role of AEMO in managing market responses. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, affecting the same day. Its root_cause is: Demand forecast adjustments and tight supply/demand balance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-02-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market, particularly SA, NSW, QLD, and less so in VIC. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and stakeholders in the NEM. Its cause type is: Operational and market response. Its causes are: Fluctuations in demand forecasts, tight supply/demand conditions, and market response notices.

title: [Post 1] Early AEMO forecasts look towards new record electricity demand in Queensland today
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early AEMO forecasts look towards new record electricity demand in Queensland today" posted at 13-02-2017 06:43:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) forecasts a new record for electricity demand in Queensland today, with an estimated peak of 9,544 MW at 17:00. This is following the high demand experienced on Sunday due to increased temperatures. NEM-Watch issued a Level of Reserve (LOR1) notice, indicating a tight supply/demand balance between 16:00 and 17:00, though no loadshedding is expected, but high electricity prices are anticipated. Political efforts are criticized for failing to effectively address the power crisis.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, lasting throughout the day. Its root cause is: High electricity demand due to extreme temperatures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-02-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Demand side. Its causes are: Unusually high temperatures leading to increased electricity demand.

title: [2nd post] Astonishing Queensland electricity demand – on a Sunday!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Astonishing Queensland electricity demand – on a Sunday!" posted at 2017-02-12 08:26:00 PM is: The news reports a notable surge in electricity demand in Queensland, Australia, on Sunday, February 12, 2017, attributed to extreme heat. The demand recorded by Powerlink reached a new all-time high at 17:30, although the NEM-Watch snapshot showed a lower peak of 9,430MW at 17:25, still a significant figure. The measurement discrepancies are due to differences in methodology, with Powerlink using a Metered Demand approach over a half-hour and NEM-Watch using a 5-minute Dispatch Target basis. Despite the differences, the high demand on a typically low consumption day indicates significant strain on the power supply. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2017-02-12. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Prolonged high temperatures leading to increased electricity demand.

title: [1st post] AEMO forecasts near-record electricity demand in Queensland – late on a Sunday evening!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecasts near-record electricity demand in Queensland – late on a Sunday evening!" posted at 12-02-2017 11:06:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has forecasted a significant electricity demand surge in Queensland, expected to peak at 9,297MW on Sunday evening, nearing the record set earlier in the summer. The demand spike comes after recent electricity supply challenges in South Australia and New South Wales, highlighting a strained grid during extreme weather conditions in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability . Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, on the evening of 12th February 2017. title: About solar panels (predominantly) facing north, not west
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About solar panels (predominantly) facing north, not west" posted at 11-02-2017 08:35:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenges faced by solar energy in the context of the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the summer of 2016-17, highlighting the limitations of north-facing solar panels in effectively reducing peak demand in the afternoons due to the "duck curve" issue. It suggests that reorienting solar panels westward and introducing battery storage could enhance solar contributions during critical times, especially given the strain observed on the energy system on February 9th and 10th. The article also refutes claims that solar power alone "saved" New South Wales during this period, emphasizing that all sources of energy generation and conservation were equally crucial in maintaining grid stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Short-term, affecting immediate response strategies during the crisis but also long-term for future solar panel orientation and energy policy. Its root_cause is: The misalignment between solar panel orientation and peak demand times, leading to less effective solar power generation when most needed. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-02-2017 and 10-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in NSW, including large industrial users like the Tomago aluminium smelter. Its cause type is: Structural and policy-related regarding solar energy deployment. Its causes are: The structural positioning of solar panels, the inherent characteristics of the "duck curve," and market-related policy considerations in the solar energy sector.

title: [Post 2] Demand climbs and smelter readies to close AGAIN
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand climbs and smelter readies to close AGAIN" posted at 11-02-2017 03:41:00 PM is: The article reports on the climbing electricity demand in New South Wales (NSW) due to a heatwave, reaching levels not seen in six years, necessitating demand response actions such as the Tomago Aluminium smelter reducing consumption to prevent system collapses or load shedding. Solar PV's mixed impact on peak demand, with small-scale systems declining rapidly while large-scale plants maintain output longer, is also discussed; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, during the heatwave. Its root cause is: Extreme weather conditions causing increased electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Tomago Aluminium smelter and potentially other large electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Natural and operational. Its causes are: Heatwave increasing demand, reduced solar PV output in small scales, demand response to prevent grid instability.

title: [Post 1] Not out of the woods yet in NSW on a super-hot Saturday
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Not out of the woods yet in NSW on a super-hot Saturday" posted at 11-02-2017 01:03:00 PM is: The news article discussed the electricity demand challenges faced by New South Wales (NSW) on a super-hot Saturday in the summer of 2016-17. With temperatures soaring, the NSW Scheduled Demand was anticipated to reach near-record levels, raising concerns over the supply-demand balance and prompting market notices from the AEMO regarding the tight electricity situation. The article highlighted the importance of a well-functioning interconnected grid and the critical role of electricity imports from neighboring regions like Victoria and Queensland in maintaining stability. Amidst the tight supply scenario, the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin was forecasted to drop, stressing the need for careful energy management and underscoring the broader industry challenges. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing during the heatwave. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand due to extremely hot weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Extreme hot weather leading to increased electricity demand, reduced solar PV injections, dependence on electricity imports.

title: Demand in NSW soars but falls short of setting new record
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Demand in NSW soars but falls short of setting new record" posted at 10-02-2017 05:53:00 PM is: On February 10, 2017, electricity demand in New South Wales (NSW) peaked at 14,108MW at 5:30pm AEDT, falling short of the state's record 14,649MW. The shortfall was influenced by load shedding at the Tomago Aluminium Smelter, which reduced demand by approximately 310MW, along with a 400MW generator failure at 5:20pm. Further load shedding possibly affected additional customers or potlines at Tomago starting at 6:00pm. Further analysis is expected in the following week; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Generator failure and load shedding. Its accident scale is: Localized. Its dates is: 10-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: Potentially includes general consumers and industrial users like Tomago Aluminium Smelter. Its cause type is: Technical failure and load management. Its causes is: 400MW generator failure and load shedding actions at Tomago Aluminium Smelter.

title: [4th update] NSW drops under 1,000MW spare capacity
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "[4th update] NSW drops under 1,000MW spare capacity" posted at 10-02-2017 03:32:00 PM is: The report indicates a critical reduction in spare generation capacity in New South Wales (NSW) below 1,000MW during a period of climbing electricity demand, with no available spare transmission capacity from neighboring states, leaving NSW isolated. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "[Update 3] NSW Scheduled Demand up past 13,500MW and Reserve Margin drops" posted at 10-02-2017 02:22:00 PM is: The article discusses the New South Wales (NSW) scheduled electricity demand reaching 13,510MW, with a forecast to peak at 14,722MW during a hot weather period in the summer of 2016-17. The Reserve Plant Margin in the QLD+NSW region is down to 12% and expected to decline further, raising concerns about grid stability. The high temperatures impact power supply by reducing the efficiency and output of thermal power stations, power line capacity, and some solar panel outputs, while increasing air-conditioning driven consumption. A market notice also highlights the constraints on the electricity grid in Victoria due to the heat; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Extreme weather (high temperatures) leading to increased electricity demand and reduced supply capability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 10-02-2017. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "[2nd update] AEMO forecast for peak NSW demand this afternoon now would be new record" posted at 10-02-2017 11:21:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has updated its predispatch forecast, predicting a potential new record for Scheduled Demand in New South Wales (NSW) of 11,734MW at 16:30 NEM time (17:30 Sydney daylight savings time). The anticipated peak is attributed to the expected decline in solar PV production later in the afternoon. The NEM-Watch snapshot indicates that this demand surge poses the risk of load shedding, although efforts will likely be made to avoid such an outcome; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Predicted high demand combined with declining solar PV output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in New South Wales. title: Early Friday 10th February – forecasts for near-record electricity demand and load shedding in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early Friday 10th February – forecasts for near-record electricity demand and load shedding in NSW" posted at 10-02-2017 06:36:00 AM is: The article reports on the National Electricity Market (NEM) facing an anticipated near-record electricity demand in New South Wales (NSW) due to extremely high temperatures. The peak demand forecasted for the afternoon of February 10th could come close to surpassing the previous recorded peak demand set in February 2011. Contributing factors to this high demand level include the hot weather leading to increased air-conditioning use and the historical closure of the Kurri Kurri aluminium smelter, which reduced base-load consumption. Despite growth in solar PV potentially offsetting some demand, warnings of reserve shortages for NSW and a tight supply/demand balance in Queensland are highlighted. The publication encourages monitoring of temperature impacts on electricity consumption across regions. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand surge. Its causes are: High temperatures, increased air-conditioning use, past closure of an aluminium smelter, solar PV netting off demand.

This situation is predicted to occur on February 10, 2017, between 15:00 to 17:00 NEM time, prompting AEMO to issue a market notice warning. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Initial Analysis: SA Load Shedding Wed 8 Feb 2017
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Initial Analysis: SA Load Shedding Wed 8 Feb 2017" posted at 09-02-2017 12:31:00 PM is: Due to extreme heat conditions in South Australia on February 8, 2017, electricity demand surged beyond supportable levels, leading to rolling controlled load shedding over a period of about one hour to maintain a secure system operation. Despite the forecast showing high demand, the situation was exacerbated by lower-than-expected wind generation and technical constraints on interconnector capacities. The high temperature reduced the output potential of wind and gas turbines, causing a gap between local supply and demand that could not be fully met by interconnector imports, prompting AEMO to instruct load shedding. There were outages at Torrens Island Power Station and Port Lincoln Gas Turbines, along with a long-term outage at Pelican Point Power Station, which reduced thermal generation capacity. The demand continued to surpass forecasts throughout the afternoon, resulting in widespread power outages amid peak temperatures.

Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, on the same day of the event. Its root_cause is: Extreme heat leading to increased demand and supply constraints. Its accident scale is: Regional, focused on South Australia. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 08-02-2017 (main event date). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Natural event influencing supply conditions. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Extreme heat, reduced wind generation, technical constraints on interconnectors, and outages at key thermal power stations.

title: AEMO forecasts LOR3 (load shedding) in NSW tomorrow–Friday 10th February
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecasts LOR3 (load shedding) in NSW tomorrow–Friday 10th February" posted at 09-02-2017 06:21:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a market notice forecasting potential load shedding in New South Wales for the time period of 17:00 to 17:30 Sydney daylight savings on Friday, 10th February 2017. This notice comes in the wake of recent load shedding in Adelaide, indicating a very tight supply and demand balance in the electricity market. Although this is only a forecast and not yet occurring, it signals a likelihood of extremely high electricity prices as the market is expected to respond by increasing available capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate/short-term. Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand balance in the electricity market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-02-2017 to 10-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecasted event. Its causes are: Tight supply-demand balance, forecasted LOR3 (load shedding), recent load shedding in Adelaide.

title: Load Shedding occurs in South Australia in heatwave conditions
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Load Shedding occurs in South Australia in heatwave conditions" posted at 08-02-2017 07:49:00 PM is: South Australia once again experienced load shedding due to heatwave conditions on February 8th, 2017, adding to previous electricity disruptions in 2016. The state's electricity reserves reached a critically low level with only 26MW of spare capacity, leading to immediate electricity demand management. Gas-fired generation was the primary electricity source, with minimal contributions from liquid-fueled plants and wind turbines struggling due to calm conditions. This incident highlights the ongoing challenges in South Australia's electricity sector, exacerbated by extreme weather and political factors. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Extreme heatwave conditions leading to high electricity demand and insufficient supply margins. Its accident scale is: Statewide. Its dates are: 08-02-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Environmental and systemic. Its causes are: Extreme heat increasing demand, insufficient generation reserves, and existing political and infrastructural challenges in the electricity sector.

title: Southern NEM demand awakes from slumber
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Southern NEM demand awakes from slumber" posted at 08-02-2017 03:45:00 PM is: The article discusses an anticipated significant rise in electricity demand in southern NEM regions due to extreme temperatures, with Adelaide reaching 42 degrees and Melbourne over 35 degrees. A spike in electricity prices in South Australia to nearly $14,000/MWh was recorded due to a temporary reduction in the Murraylink DC interconnector export limit, with further price increases expected. AEMO forecasts indicate that electricity demand will peak at over 11,700 MW, the highest in three years, which is expected to drive price spikes. Allan O'Neil, an experienced energy markets consultant, provides insights into these developments. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Extreme temperatures causing high electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-02-2017. Its affected region is: Southern NEM, including South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia and Victoria. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand increase. Its causes are: High temperatures, reduction in interconnector capacity, predicted electricity demand spike.

title: The ‘other’ factors driving price volatility in the NEM
author: Gilles Walgenwitz
classified_content: The summary of the news "The ‘other’ factors driving price volatility in the NEM" posted at 08-02-2017 12:10:00 PM is: The article discusses several contributing factors to the increased price volatility in the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the summer of 2016-17. Key factors include the declining trend in gas-powered generation due to rising domestic gas prices, leading to reduced gas-fired power generation. This has caused increased volatility in the futures markets across all NEM jurisdictions, as indicated by the expansion of Bollinger Bands, which measure market volatility. Additionally, liquidity in exchange-traded electricity derivatives has been decreasing, which may result in less market transparency and a higher risk of spot-market price volatility. The domestic gas market's continued tightening is another contributing factor. Consumers are advised to develop strategies to hedge against these risks. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium term. Its root_cause is: Declining gas-powered generation and tightening domestic gas market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-02-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Large energy users, industrial consumers. Its cause type is: Market and supply-related. Its causes are: Declining gas-fired generation, rising gas prices, increased market volatility, decreased liquidity in traded volumes.

title: Overview: NSW & Qld Price Volatility, Monday 6 Feb 2017
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Overview: NSW & Qld Price Volatility, Monday 6 Feb 2017" posted at 07-02-2017 10:40:00 AM is: The article discusses the price volatility experienced in the electricity markets of New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland on 6 February 2017 due to extreme heat conditions, leading to high electricity demand. Both regions exhibited closely correlated spot price behaviors, attributed to the reliance on interconnectors like the Queensland-NSW Interconnector (QNI) and the Victorian interconnector for electricity imports during peak demand. Although pre-dispatch forecasts predicted significant volatility, the actual price fluctuations were less severe, possibly due to financial hedging strategies by Queensland generators. The article also mentions the role of local generation availability and system interconnectors in managing electricity supply during periods of high demand.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term, focused on the day of 6 February 2017. Root_cause is: Extreme heat conditions leading to high electricity demand. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates are: 07-02-2017. Affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland, Australia. Affected key users are: Unknown. Cause type is: Weather-related demand surge. Causes are: Extreme heat, high electricity demand, capacity reliance on interconnectors, financial hedging by generators.

title: NSW demand rises above 13,000MW
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW demand rises above 13,000MW" posted at 06-02-2017 01:41:00 PM is: Demand in New South Wales spiked above 13,000MW on February 6, 2017, despite moderate temperatures in its capital, reaching 13,331MW by early afternoon. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as it pertains to immediate demand changes. Its root_cause is: Rising electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-02-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Demand-related. Its causes are: Increased electricity demand in New South Wales amid moderate summer temperatures.

title: Overview: Queensland volatility 1-2 Feb 2017
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Overview: Queensland volatility 1-2 Feb 2017" posted at 03-02-2017 02:00:00 PM is: The article discusses volatile electricity pricing in Queensland over February 1-2, 2017, driven by high weather-related demand and a limited generator bidstack. Prices exceeded $1,000/MWh in multiple intervals, with average daily prices reaching $406/MWh and $781/MWh for those days. Demand response was evident, as price spikes prompted reductions in demand, mitigating some price increases. Despite this, high prices persisted, especially during afternoon peak demand periods due to insufficient demand reduction.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate impact during February 1-2, 2017. Its root cause is: Weather-driven demand and limited generation bidstack. Its accident scale is: Not applicable/Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2017 to 02-02-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown, but likely large consumers and market participants. Its cause type is: Market reaction due to pricing pressures. Its causes are: Weather-driven demand, limited supply offers, price-responsive demand reductions.

title: Initial Review: NEM-wide Price Event, Monday 30 Jan 2017
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Initial Review: NEM-wide Price Event, Monday 30 Jan 2017" posted at 31-01-2017 02:25:00 PM is: On January 30, 2017, a sudden spike in spot prices occurred in the three largest regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM), namely New South Wales (NSW), Queensland, and Victoria, due largely to extremely hot weather and a series of transmission constraints. Brutally high temperatures, reaching 44 degrees Celsius in parts of NSW, led to increased electricity demand. Compounding the issue, transmission constraints limited the flow of generation from Victoria to NSW, resulting in a brief period of high prices. Additionally, strategic generator rebidding at critical moments further influenced price spikes, with prices topping $13,999/MWh in some regions. Subsequently, prices plummeted due to generators rebidding more capacity at lower prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, with prices returning to normal after the event. Its root cause is: Extreme hot weather conditions leading to high power demand and transmission bottlenecks. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-01-2017. Its affected region is: Primarily New South Wales, with effects also in Queensland and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM regions. Its cause type is: Environmental and operational. Its causes are: Extreme heat causing high demand, transmission constraints, and generator rebidding behavior.

title: Quick Review: $13,800/MWh Queensland spike Mon Jan 30
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quick Review: $13,800/MWh Queensland spike Mon Jan 30" posted at 31-01-2017 09:40:00 AM is: The article discusses an isolated five-minute electricity price spike in Queensland on January 30, 2017, which reached $13,800/MWh. The spike was attributed to demand fluctuations, interconnector constraints, and a likely rebid of coal-fired and gas-fired generator outputs. These actions prompted the dispatch of high-price bid bands due to the constraints on interconnectors from NSW and variations in demand. The author anticipates further clarification with the publication of Tuesday’s bid data to confirm these preliminary findings.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, affecting prices shortly after the spike at 06:50. Its root_cause is: Constraints on interconnectors and generator rebidding. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-01-2017 (same as the event date). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Market and system operational issues. Its causes are: Interconnector flow limits, demand fluctuations, and generator rebidding of outputs.

title: Initial Review: $14,000/MWh Spike in Qld, Fri 27 Jan 2017
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Initial Review: $14,000/MWh Spike in Qld, Fri 27 Jan 2017" posted at 29-01-2017 07:58:00 PM is: The article reviews an extreme price spike in Queensland's electricity market on 27 January 2017, where prices soared to $14,000/MWh for a 5-minute dispatch interval at 10:35 AM. This spike resulted from constrained interconnector flows from New South Wales, which limited Queensland's ability to import power, combined with a "thin" generation bid stack that forced reliance on high-priced bids from local generators. Though this led to perceptions of potential shortfalls, there was adequate total capacity with lower-priced options unavailable or offline. The 5-minute spike's financial impact was exacerbated by the "5/30" market pricing mechanism, resulting in a half-hour settlement price of approximately $2,353/MWh. This situation incentivized generators to rebid at lower prices and loads to reduce demand, subsequently altering market dynamics for the half-hour. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: Constrained interconnector flows and inadequate low-priced generation bids. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-01-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Local electricity consumers and market participants. Its cause type is: Market operation issue. Its causes are: Constrained interconnector from NSW, high market demand, and limited availability of low-priced generation bids.

title: Review of the NEM in 2016
author: Ric Brazalle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Review of the NEM in 2016" posted at 25-01-2017 11:32:00 AM is: The article reviews the changes in electricity consumption and generation within the National Electricity Market (NEM) for 2016 compared to 2015. There was an increase in electricity consumption by 0.8%, with Queensland and NSW seeing rises, while other states experienced reductions. Solar energy and energy efficiency contributed to a reduction in consumption, accounting for a 1% decline. Renewable energy, led by hydro, wind, and solar, increased its market share from 12.2% to 14.7%, significantly impacting greenhouse gas emissions, which fell by 1.3%. The shift in generation included a drop in gas-fired generation and brown coal, replaced by renewables and black coal. Key projects included the start-up of several large renewable facilities and the closure of the Northern Power Station. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout 2016. Its root_cause is: Shifts in energy consumption patterns and energy generation mix due to increased renewable adoption and efficiency measures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-01-2017. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market and policy-driven. Its causes are: Increase in renewable energy, energy efficiency measures, reduction in gas-fired and brown coal generation, closure of the Northern Power Station.

title: A more forensic look at Queensland Price Volatility on Saturday 14th January 2017
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A more forensic look at Queensland Price Volatility on Saturday 14th January 2017" posted at 24-01-2017 11:33:00 AM is: The article provides an in-depth analysis of the significant electricity price volatility experienced in Queensland on Saturday, 14th January 2017, highlighting the impact of extreme weather and high demand due to peak temperatures. The volatility led to multiple trading periods with prices soaring above $1000/MWh, with some periods exceeding $5000/MWh, prompting an upcoming investigation by the AER. The price spikes were influenced by factors such as gas prices, solar contribution decline, and market competition dynamics. Moreover, unhedged capacity and the effect of these events on future retail prices were also discussed, along with the implications for Queensland's renewable energy target by 2030. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate short-term impact on spot market prices with potential medium-to-long term influence on retail contracts. Its root_cause is: High demand from extreme weather conditions and market dynamics involving supply constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14th January 2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Large energy users and manufacturers with spot market exposure. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand spike and market dynamics. Its causes are: Peak temperatures leading to high demand, reduced solar PV contribution, and limited competition among major suppliers.

title: One example of how price spikes in the physical market flow through to the financial market
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "One example of how price spikes in the physical market flow through to the financial market" posted at 24-01-2017 08:59:00 AM is: The news article discusses the volatility in the wholesale physical market of the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced on January 14th, 2017, and how such volatility can impact future contract prices. It explains that volatility induces traders and energy users to hedge more, driving up contract prices. The article also references historical cases where retailers like Jackgreen, EnergyOne, and GoEnergy have gone bust due, in part, to ineffective hedging strategies exposed by market volatility. An analysis of the trend in Queensland shows significant increases in the traded prices of the Q1 2017 futures contract and a notable, although smaller, impact on the prices for Q1 2018 and Q1 2019. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term (up to a few years, as indicated by futures Q1 2017 - Q1 2019). Its root_cause is: Volatility in the physical (spot) market on January 14th, 2017. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-01-2017 for the market event, and 24-01-2017 for the publication. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Traders, energy users, and wholesale market customers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Market sentiment and reactions to physical market volatility. Its causes are: Spot market volatility, increased demand for hedging, ineffective hedging strategies leading to financial distress for retailers.

title: Illustrating how the currently installed rooftop solar PV has reduced peak demand – but more won’t reduce it more**
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Illustrating how the currently installed rooftop solar PV has reduced peak demand – but more won’t reduce it more" posted at 24-01-2017 08:49:00 AM is: The installed rooftop solar PV has effectively reduced the peak electricity demand, particularly noted on specific days in January 2017 in Queensland. For example, without the solar PV, peak demands on these days would have exceeded 9,000 to 9,500 MW. However, further installations of similar solar PVs will not reduce peak demand further due to the peak demand timing shifting to late afternoons when solar outputs drop (i.e., the "duck curve" challenge). This highlights the need for changes in incentives, like promoting west-facing panels to potentially align solar output with peak demand times. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as solar PV installations have immediate effects on peak demand but future effects depend on policy changes. Its root_cause is: The increased deployment of rooftop solar PV panels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-01-2017 to 15-01-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and the grid system in Queensland. Its cause type is: Technological and policy-based. Its causes are: Current incentive structures, north-facing solar installations, and the resulting changes in electricity demand profiles.

title: Queensland demand today exceeds the previous all-time record
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland demand today exceeds the previous all-time record" posted at 18-01-2017 03:41:00 PM is: The Queensland region experienced a significant rise in electricity demand, surpassing the previous all-time record, driven largely by a very hot afternoon. Demand peaked at 9,477MW, requiring increased gas-fired generation, which in turn led to a spike in gas prices at the Brisbane hub to $13.39/GJ. This event coincided with a decline in solar PV injections by 600MW and a 200MW demand response to a spot price surge above $2000/MWh.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: Increased demand driven by very hot weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 18-01-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather and demand-driven. Its causes are: Hot weather leading to increased electricity demand, a decrease in solar PV output, and high spot and gas prices.

title: [UPDATED] NEM-wide demand screams past 34,000MW today
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "[UPDATED] NEM-wide demand screams past 34,000MW today" posted at 17-01-2017 03:37:00 PM is: The NEM-wide electricity demand soared past 34,000MW, marking a significant rise and highlighting potential supply challenges, especially with the upcoming closure of the Hazelwood power station. Demand peaks in both NSW and QLD contributed to the low IRPM of 11.63%, a key indicator of reduced supply cushion. Despite being lower than past records, the high demand reflects the critical pressures likely to impact electricity market stability and prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand and impending power station closure. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates is: 17-01-2017 03:37:00 PM. Its affected region is: NEM-wide, particularly NSW and QLD. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Systemic event due to high demand. Its causes is: High NEM-wide demand exceeding 34,000MW, low IRPM, demand peaks in NSW/QLD, potential impacts from Hazelwood closure.

title: QLD’s Cumulative Price over half way to the Cumulative Price Threshold after a “sizzling Saturday”
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD’s Cumulative Price over half way to the Cumulative Price Threshold after a “sizzling Saturday”" posted at 14-01-2017 05:27:00 PM is: This article discusses how the hot weather in southern Queensland during the weekend has increased electricity demand and prices substantially, causing the Queensland Cumulative Price to rise significantly above half the set Cumulative Price Threshold (CPT) of $210,000 for the 2016-17 financial year. If this threshold is reached, AEMO is required to impose price caps in Queensland temporarily. The article highlights past instances when the CPT was reached in other regions and warns of continued volatility throughout the summer. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing throughout the summer. Its root cause is: Hot weather conditions resulting in increased electricity demand and prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-01-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Increased temperatures leading to higher demand and price volatility in the electricity market.

title: … meanwhile, down at Loy Yang A…
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "… meanwhile, down at Loy Yang A…" posted at 13-01-2017 06:02:00 PM is: There have been ongoing tensions at the Loy Yang A power station involving a dispute between AGL Energy and unions, with the Fair Work Commission terminating the existing Enterprise Bargaining Agreement (EBA). This raises the potential for mass resignations, which could lead to operational disruptions at the plant amid forecasts of high temperatures in Victoria, likely increasing electricity demand. Although the potential pay cuts would not occur until May 1st, the situation could align with the upcoming Hazelwood closure, accentuating the impact on the electricity market.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (pending possible resignations and corresponding operational impact around May). Its root_cause is: Termination of the EBA and subsequent industrial dispute at Loy Yang A. Its accident scale is: Industrial dispute potential (no immediate accident). Its dates are: 13-01-2017 (and potential impact around May 2017). Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Industrial Relations. Its causes are: Termination of the EBA, potential for industrial action, possible mass resignations, policy-related coal-fired power station operation issues.

title: Queensland demand peaks even higher on Friday – and Reserve Plant Margin is tight
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland demand peaks even higher on Friday – and Reserve Plant Margin is tight" posted at 13-01-2017 05:29:00 PM is: On January 13, 2017, Queensland experienced a high demand for electricity exceeding 9,000MW due to extreme hot weather, resulting in tight supply-demand conditions and causing the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) to trigger a warning. The decline in solar PV injections during the afternoon, combined with increased air-conditioning use, further strained the grid. The dispatch price reached as high as the Market Price Cap of $14,000/MWh, near the all-time demand record set in February 2016. The summary highlights the impact of energy policy incentives, particularly the 44c FIT, which contributed to solar PV decline when demand peaked. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate during the afternoon peak hours on January 13, 2017. Its root_cause is: Extreme hot weather coupled with declining solar PV output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 13-01-2017. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial users with high air-conditioning demand. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand and solar PV output reduction. Its causes are: High temperatures, increased air-conditioning use, and reduced afternoon solar PV output.

title: Queensland electricity demand surpasses 9000MW in heatwave
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland electricity demand surpasses 9000MW in heatwave" posted at 12-01-2017 03:54:00 PM is: During the summer heatwave on January 12, 2017, Queensland's electricity demand surged past 9,000MW for the first time in the season, reaching 9,033MW at 15:30. This spike coincided with high electricity prices, peaking at $13,650.01/MWh for the 5-minute dispatch period. The demand increase was attributed to hot weather shifting from NSW to Queensland, affecting energy consumption. The ongoing afternoon decline in small-scale solar PV injections also contributed to the rising scheduled demand, highlighting potential for further demand increases in the following months.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate impact on demand and prices during the heatwave). Its root_cause is: Extreme heat due to weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-01-2017. Its affected region is: Southern Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Heatwave in southern Queensland, reduced solar PV output, increased electricity demand, and high electricity prices.

title: Hot weather in NSW drives demand higher
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Hot weather in NSW drives demand higher" posted at 11-01-2017 02:20:00 PM is: The rising temperatures in New South Wales (NSW) have led to an increase in electricity demand, pushing it into the orange zone as observed in the National Electricity Market (NEM). On January 11th, 2017, at 13:15 NEM time, the Scheduled Demand climbed to 12,550MW and continued to rise to 12,840MW by 14:50. This surge in demand can be attributed to the fading impact of small-scale solar energy contributions due to current incentives favoring north-facing solar panel installations over west-facing ones, resulting in a "duck curve" effect. Demand in Queensland was also noted to be in the orange zone at 8,440MW at the same time; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term during the summer period. Its root_cause is: Increased demand due to hot weather and limited solar PV impact in the afternoon. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-01-2017. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Rising temperatures, reduced solar PV output in the afternoon due to north-facing installations, and increased electricity consumption.

title: Victorian “Scheduled Demand” plunges to its lowest ever* level – early on Monday 2nd January
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victorian 'Scheduled Demand' plunges to its lowest ever level – early on Monday 2nd January" posted at 03-01-2017 12:56:00 PM is: The article reports on an unprecedented drop in Victorian electricity demand, which fell below 3,000MW for the first time, coinciding with lower demand in NSW. This trend, persisting since December 1st, is partly attributed to technical issues at Alcoa's Portland Aluminium smelter following a transmission outage. The demand dip raises concerns about future electricity market dynamics.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Since December 2016 onwards. Its root_cause is: Technical issues at Alcoa’s Portland Aluminium smelter and a transmission outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-01-2017. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Industrial users such as Alcoa’s Portland Aluminium smelter. Its cause type is: Multiple causes. Its causes are: Technical issues at an aluminum smelter and a transmission outage.

title: SA electricity demand at New Year’s exhibiting the “Duck Curve” affliction
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "SA electricity demand at New Year’s exhibiting the 'Duck Curve' affliction" posted at 01-01-2017 04:18:00 PM is: During the New Year period, South Australia's electricity demand showcased characteristics of the "Duck Curve" phenomenon, indicating extreme dips in daytime demand levels. On New Year's Day, scheduled demand in South Australia dropped notably, with the dispatch price nearing $0/MWh, influenced by factors like post-New Year’s celebrations, moderate weather, and increased solar power reducing daytime consumption. The situation led to the shutting down of Pelican Point and reliance on Torrens and interconnectors for FCAS services. This occurrence underlined the ongoing complexities in the electricity market, driven by the energy transition, as well as concerns about system security and economics amidst evolving technological interventions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: System instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing as the demand patterns underscore challenges with peak load management. Its root_cause is: Reduction in daytime electricity demand due to increased solar generation and moderate weather. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-01-2017. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity producers and grid operators in South Australia. Its cause type is: Demand-supply imbalance due to renewable energy integration. Its causes are: New Year's holiday effects, mild weather conditions, and high solar energy penetration leading to decreased operation of traditional thermal plants.
